<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141370 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00193: Comment

- page_id: `189141370`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141370/Comment
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:08.234+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A **Comment** is a UML element used to specify various remarks of an element in the model. In the diagram palette, under **Common**, the Comment command appears under the Note command. You can display text in the Comment as plain or HTML text.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a comment.

You can format the Comment's symbol properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

You can specify a Comment in the Comment's [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''], which also displays the description of each Comment property in the description area of the Specification window.

To create a comment in the element Specification window

1. Open the Specification window of the selected element.
2. In the panel on the left, select the Documentation/Comments property group.
3. Under the Comments pane, click Add . The Comment's Specification window opens. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Type the Comment content as the **Body** property value and click **OK**.
5. Close the Specification window.

To view the **Documentation/Comments** symbol on the diagram pane, expand the**Documentation/Comments**property group and drag the selected documentation/comment on the diagram pane.

To switch a comment to HTML or Plain Text

Do one of the following:

- On the diagram pane, select the Comment symbol. Click the button that appears on the lower-left corner of the shape:
  - The HTML button - to switch the text to the HTML text. The Plain button - to switch the text to the Plain text. [ATTACHMENT filename='comment_switch_to_html.png']

#### TIP: HTML text

HTML text

You can edit the HTML text in the comment using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='HTML editor' space=''].

To display an anchored element documentation in a comment

1. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Anchor' space=''] a Comment to a symbol on the diagram pane.
2. Select the Comment on the diagram pane. Open its shortcut menu.
3. Select the Retrieve Documentation command. The anchored symbol's documentation is displayed on the Comment’s symbol.

#### TIP: Retrieve Documentation

Retrieve Documentation

You can enable the **Retrieve Documentation** option **only** if you already have documentation in the Comment's Specification window. Read the first set of instructions on this page to see how to create the comment in the element's Specification window.

#### TIP: Edit

Edit

You can edit element documentation directly on the Comment symbol.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A <strong>Comment</strong> is a UML element used to specify various remarks of an element in the model. In the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7ada6c7a-9164-40b6-a89e-ada926cefcad">diagram palette</ac:inline-comment-marker>, under <strong>Common</strong>, the Comment command appears under the Note command. You can display text in the Comment as plain or HTML text.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="comment.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a comment.</h6><p>You can format the Comment's symbol properties in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Formatting symbols" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>You can specify a Comment in the Comment's <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>, which also displays the description of each Comment property in the description area of the Specification window.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a comment in the element Specification window</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Specification window of the selected element.</li><li>In the panel on the left, select the <strong>Documentation/Comments </strong>property group.</li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="32666a56-71d4-4dfc-813e-e12235819c11">Under the Comments pane, click </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="32666a56-71d4-4dfc-813e-e12235819c11">Add</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="32666a56-71d4-4dfc-813e-e12235819c11">. The Comment's Specification window opens.</ac:inline-comment-marker><br /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="32666a56-71d4-4dfc-813e-e12235819c11"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="docummentation_comments_dialog.png" /></ac:image><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><p>Type the Comment content as the <strong>Body</strong> property value and click <strong>OK</strong>.</p></li><li>Close the Specification window.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="97427e0f-6b33-4e13-a3f2-1ece8761d2f3"><ac:rich-text-body><p><span>To view the <strong>Documentation/Comments</strong> symbol on the diagram pane, expand the </span><strong>Documentation/Comments</strong><span> property group and drag the selected documentation/comment on the diagram pane. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />To switch a comment to HTML or Plain Text</p><hr /><p>Do one of the following:</p><ul><li>On the diagram pane, select the Comment symbol. Click the button that appears on the lower-left corner of the shape:<ul><li>The <strong>HTML</strong> button - to switch the text to the HTML text.<br />The <strong>Plain</strong> button - to switch the text to the Plain text.<br /><ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="111"><ri:attachment ri:filename="comment_switch_to_html.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li></ul></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="471f6d9b-6129-4147-ad25-9f84cece96fa"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">HTML text</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can edit the HTML text in the comment using the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="HTML editor" /></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>To display an anchored element documentation in a comment</p><hr /><ol><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Anchor" /></ac:link> a Comment to a symbol on the diagram pane.</li><li>Select the Comment on the diagram pane. Open its shortcut menu.</li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="30d0409a-5445-4345-93e0-f4cc22f8786b">Select the </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="30d0409a-5445-4345-93e0-f4cc22f8786b">Retrieve Documentation</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="30d0409a-5445-4345-93e0-f4cc22f8786b"> command. </ac:inline-comment-marker><br />The anchored symbol's documentation is displayed on the Comment’s symbol.</li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="80451aa7-c458-4c7e-8de8-9e8483d6d683"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Retrieve Documentation</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can enable the <strong>Retrieve Documentation</strong> option <strong>only</strong> if you already have documentation in the Comment's Specification window. Read the first set of instructions on this page to see how to create the comment in the element's Specification window.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fefe8eed-17e3-48d0-b388-18215fa615dc"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Edit</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can edit element documentation directly on the Comment symbol.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145051 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00194: Comment and unparsing code

- page_id: `189145051`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145051/Comment+and+unparsing+code
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:25.745+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Velocity templating language
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### Comment

Comment is used for explaining or making notes regarding the code. You can include text comments in the report. The report engine will not parse comments.

To make a single line comment

- Type **##** before the comment. The report engine will skip all the text in the line, as follows:

To make multi-line comments

- Type **#*** followed by the multi-line comments and end them with ***#**. The report engine will skip all the lines between these symbols, as follows:

#### Unparsing code

You can declare the code in Velocity template without parsing it into the report. The report engine will not parse the code.

To unparse code

- Type **#[[** followed by the code to unparse and end it with **]]#**, as follows: #[[
 session_start();
 YOUR CODE HERE
 ?
 ]]#

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<h2>Comment</h2><p>Comment is used for explaining or making notes regarding the code. You can include text comments in the report. The report engine will not parse comments.</p><p><br /></p><p>To make a single line comment</p><hr /><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type <strong>##</strong> before the comment. The report engine will skip all the text in the line, as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ded1f61e-1866-4f15-bf90-58c03653b281"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[##Comment here]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul><p>To make multi-line comments</p><hr /><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type <strong>#*</strong> followed by the multi-line comments and end them with <strong>*#</strong>.  The report engine will skip all the lines between these symbols, as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a2e93bf9-6bbf-4e48-860c-3594bf830819"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#*
	Comment line1
	Comment line2
	Comment line3
*#]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul><p><br /></p><h2>Unparsing code</h2><p>You can declare the code in Velocity template without parsing it into the report. The report engine will not parse the code.</p><p><br /></p><p>To unparse code</p><hr /><ul><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type <strong>#[[</strong> followed by the code to unparse and end it with <strong>]]#</strong>, as follows:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="c739a728-55e5-4f10-b714-22838c6ba8f2"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[<html>
	<body>
		#[[
			<?php
			session_start();
			YOUR CODE HERE
			?>
		]]#
	</body>
</html>]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=209257406 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00195: Committing changes to 3DEXPERIENCE platform

- page_id: `209257406`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/209257406/Committing+changes+to+3DEXPERIENCE+platform
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-02-04T14:54:10.171+01:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Collaboration powered by 3DEXPERIENCE platform
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

After you finish editing a server project, you must commit the changes to the **3D**EXPERIENCEplatform so that other users working on the same project would be able to see them. When you commit project changes, new project data from your modeling tool is merged with the project stored on the**server**. As a result, a new project iterationis created.

To commit project changes to the **3D**EXPERIENCEplatform

1. In the main menu of a modeling tool, select Collaborate > Commit Changes to Server .
2. In the **Comment** box of the open dialog, optionally describe the model changes.
3. If you want to keep the locks on all diagrams, leave the option Keep locks selected.
4. If you want to view the local changes you are about to commit, click the**More**button. In the**Local Changes**tab, added elements are highlighted in green, deleted elements are highlighted in red, and modified or moved elements are highlighted in blue. [ATTACHMENT filename='commiting_project_to_server.png']
5. Click the Commit button.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

When you commit project changes, new project data from your modeling tool is merged with the project stored on the </span><span style="color: rgb(55,65,81);"><strong>server</strong></span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. As a result, a new project iteration </span><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">is created.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">To commit project changes to the <span style="color: rgb(55,65,81);"><strong>3D</strong>EXPERIENCE </span>platform</span></p><hr /><ol><li>In the main menu of a modeling tool, select <strong>Collaborate</strong> &gt; <strong>Commit Changes to Server</strong>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">In the <strong>Comment</strong> box of the open dialog, optionally describe the model changes.</p></li><li>If you want to keep the locks on all diagrams, leave the option <strong>Keep locks </strong>selected.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span>If you want to view the local changes you are about to commit, click the </span><strong>More</strong><span> button</span><span>. In the </span><strong>Local Changes</strong><span> tab, added elements are highlighted in green, deleted elements are highlighted in red, and modified or moved elements are highlighted in blue.</span><br /><br /></p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="commiting_project_to_server.png" /></ac:image></li><li>Click the <strong>Commit</strong> button.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189143668 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00196: Committing changes to Teamwork Cloud

- page_id: `189143668`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189143668/Committing+changes+to+Teamwork+Cloud
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:40:18.737+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Collaborative modeling > Using Teamwork Cloud
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**On this page**

4

After you finish editing a model, you need to commit the changes to the server so that other team members can see these changes when editing other parts of the model. When you commit changes to the server, new data from your modeling tool is merged with the project on the server repository. As a result, a new version of that project is created. Other team members must [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Updating changes from Teamwork Cloud' space=''] from the server to see the committed changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The workflow of modifying and committing server projects.

To commit a project to Teamwork Cloud

1. In the main menu. select Collaborate > Commit Changes To Server (or press Ctrl+K).
2. If you want to describe the changes you made, enter a comment in the **Comment** box of the **Commit Changes to the Server**dialog. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] above the **Comment** box to choose a comment from the recently used comments list.
3. If you want to keep locks on model elements, select the **Keep locks** check box. Your choice will be remembered. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Optionally, click More in the lower left corner of the dialog and do one or both the following:
  - .
  - .
5. Click the Commit button.

While the project is being committed, you will see a dialog with a progress bar and your position in the commit queue as shown below.

When multiple projects are committed to the server simultaneously, all users are put in a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) line resulting in quicker and more predictable project commit execution. If putting project commits in a queue does not work for your organization, your system administrator can for a particular Teamwork Cloud server.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The commit progress dialog with your position in the commit queue.

##### Creating version tags

You can create tags for a project version when you commit it to the server. Version tags provide valuable information when [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Reviewing historical versions of the project' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Branching projects' space='']. You can also use tags to trigger notifications when used project versions with specific tags are committed to the server. To learn more, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Changing the used project version' space=''].

To create a version tag and commit a project

1. In the main menu. select Collaborate > Commit Changes To Server (or press Ctrl+K).
2. In the lower left corner of the Commit Changes to the Server dialog, click More .
3. In the Version Tags tab, click the Add button.
4. In the open dialog, type the tag text and click OK . The tag is added to the Version Tags tab. You create several tags for one project version.Select the **Create major version** check box, to add the 'Major' tag to the project version. [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_version_tags.png']
5. Click the Commit button.

##### Reviewing local changes before committing a project

You can review the local changes you have made to the project before committing it to the server.

To review local changes and commit a project

1. In the main menu. select Collaborate > Commit Changes To Server (or press Ctrl+K).
2. In the lower left corner of the Commit Changes to the Server dialog, click More .
3. Select the Local Changes tab and review local changes made to the project. Added elements are highlighted in green, deleted elements are highlighted in red, and modified or moved elements are highlighted in blue. [ATTACHMENT filename='reviewing_local_changes.png']
4. If you want to explore the changes of a certain element in more detail, select an element, click [ATTACHMENT filename='quick_diff.png'] on the right side of the Local Changes tab, then select one of the following options:
  - Quick Diff - to quickly inspect the changes of element property values (such as documentation, type, or multiplicity).
  - Full Diff - to inspect the changes made to diagrams or element property values in more detail, as well as look at the properties of new relationships and directly related elements (such as new or modified attributes and operations).
  - Compare projects - to compare local project changes to the latest project version on the server. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing comparison results' space=''] .
5. Click the Commit button.

##### Disabling commit queues

If putting project commits in a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) line does not work for your organization, your system administrator candisable the commit queue mechanismfor a particular Teamwork Cloud server.

To disable commit queues

1. Open for editing the <install_root>/TeamworkCloud/configuration/application.conf file.
2. Add the following line to the file:
3. Restart the Teamwork Cloud server.

If putting project commits in a queue does not work for your organization, your system administrator can <ac:link ac:anchor="Disabling commit queues"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[disable the commit queue mechanism]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for a particular Teamwork Cloud server.</span></span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="commit_queues.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">The commit progress dialog with your position in the commit queue.</h6><h3>Creating version tags</h3><p>You can create tags for a project version when you commit it to the server. You can also use tags to trigger notifications when used project versions with specific tags are committed to the server. To learn more, see <ac:link ac:anchor="Setting notifications about version changes"><ri:page ri:content-title="Changing the used project version" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Setting notifications about version changes]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a version tag and commit a project</p><hr /><ol><li><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">In the main menu. select </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Collaborate</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> &gt; </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Commit Changes To Server</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> (or press Ctrl+K).</span></li><li>In the lower left corner of the <strong>Commit Changes to the Server </strong>dialog, click <strong>More</strong>.</li><li>In the <strong>Version </strong><strong>Tags</strong> tab, click the <strong>Add</strong> button.</li><li>In the open dialog, type the tag text and click <strong>OK</strong>. The tag is added to the <strong>Version Tags</strong> tab.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a3809347-b917-45e9-840b-3a87d6cedeea"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>You create several tags for one project version.</li><li>Select the <strong>Create major version</strong> check box, to add the 'Major' tag to the project version.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_version_tags.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>Click the <strong>Commit</strong> button.</li></ol><h3>Reviewing local changes before committing a project</h3><p>You can review the local changes you have made to the project before committing it to the server.</p><p><br /></p><p>To review local changes and commit a project</p><hr /><ol><li><span>In the main menu. select </span><strong>Collaborate</strong><span> &gt; </span><strong>Commit Changes To Server</strong><span> (or press Ctrl+K).</span></li><li>In the lower left corner of the <strong>Commit Changes to the Server </strong>dialog, click <strong>More</strong>.</li><li>Select the <strong>Local Changes</strong> tab and review local changes made to the project.<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="951aec04-8587-47fa-b78e-7cb975b192ab"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Added elements are highlighted in green, deleted elements are highlighted in red, and modified or moved elements are highlighted in blue.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="reviewing_local_changes.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li>If you want to explore the changes of a certain element in more detail, select an element, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="18"><ri:attachment ri:filename="quick_diff.png" /></ac:image> on the right side of the <strong>Local Changes</strong> tab, then select one of the following options:<ul><li><strong>Quick Diff</strong> - <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">to quickly inspect the changes of element property values (such as documentation, type, or multiplicity).</span></li><li><strong>Full Diff</strong> - <span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">to inspect the changes made to diagrams or element property values in </span>more detail,<span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><span> </span>as well as look at the properties of new relationships and directly related elements (such as new or modified attributes and operations).</span></li><li><strong>Compare projects</strong> - to compare local project changes to the latest project version on the server. <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Analyzing comparison results" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Learn how to analyze comparison results]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul></li><li>Click the <strong>Commit</strong> button.</li></ol><h3>Disabling commit queues</h3><p><span style="color: rgb(13,13,13);">If putting project commits in a First-In-First-Out (FIFO) line does not work for your organization, your system administrator can<span> </span></span>disable the commit queue mechanism<span style="color: rgb(13,13,13);"><span> </span>for a particular Teamwork Cloud server.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><span style="color: rgb(13,13,13);">To disable commit queues</span></p><hr /><ol><li>Open for editing the <em>&lt;install_root&gt;/TeamworkCloud/configuration/application.conf</em> file.</li><li>Add the following line to the file:<br /><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="30b9d2ac-aec2-45c9-b57a-4de7fe17fe23"><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[esi.persistence.queuecommit.enable = false]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li><li>Restart the Teamwork Cloud server.</li></ol></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140195 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00197: Communication diagram

- page_id: `189140195`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140195/Communication+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:45.433+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

307612157

307612159

307612158

**Overview**

A Communication diagram is a type of Interaction diagram, which falls under the behavioral diagramming family.The Communication diagram is the UML 2.0 version of the Collaboration diagram. It illustrates various static connections between objects, models, and their interactions. It also presents a collaboration that contains a set of instances as well as their required relationships given in a particular context, and includes an interaction that defines a set of messages. These messages specify the interaction between the classifier roles within a collaboration that will serve to achieve the desired result.

A Communication diagram is given in two different forms: at the instance level or at the specification level.

**Purpose**

A Communication diagram can be used to model the interactions between objects or parts in terms of sequenced messages. It represents a combination of information taken from [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space=''] diagrams describing both the static structure and dynamic behavior of a system.

**Usage**

A Communication diagram can be used to:

- model message passing between objects or roles that deliver functionalities of Use Cases and operations
- capture interactions that show the messages passed between objects and roles
- model alternative scenarios within Use Cases or operations that involve the collaboration of different objects and interactions
- model mechanisms within the architectural design of the system
- identify objects, their attributes, and operations that participate in Use Cases.

**Summary**

Communication diagrams are valuable because they:

- reveal the structural requirements that are necessary to complete a task
- explicitly identify the objects that participate in an interaction
- reveal the interface requirements of the participating classes
- identify the structural changes required to satisfy an interaction
- identify data that passed as part of interactions.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Communication diagram

307612155

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d3ab83ff-1a3e-429e-9683-86e0c0a9d355"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">307612157</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ce35aaac-9e33-46e8-95c9-48bc5aae367c"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">307612159</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bc1f253f-c1f6-44f6-86d6-44da06833a7d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">307612158</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Overview</strong></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">A Communication diagram is a type of Interaction diagram, which falls under the behavioral diagramming family. </span>The Communication diagram is the UML 2.0 version of the Collaboration diagram. It illustrates various static connections between objects, models, and their interactions. It also presents a collaboration that contains a set of instances as well as their required relationships given in a particular context, and includes an interaction that defines a set of messages. These messages specify the interaction between the classifier roles within a collaboration that will serve to achieve the desired result.</p><p>A Communication diagram is given in two different forms: at the instance level or at the specification level.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>A Communication diagram can be used to model the interactions between objects or parts in terms of sequenced messages. It represents a combination of information taken from <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Class diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Class]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Sequence]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, and <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Use Case]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> diagrams describing both the static structure and dynamic behavior of a system. </p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>A Communication diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>model message passing between objects or roles that deliver functionalities of Use Cases and operations</li><li>capture interactions that show the messages passed between objects and roles</li><li>model alternative scenarios within Use Cases or operations that involve the collaboration of different objects and interactions</li><li>model mechanisms within the architectural design of the system</li><li>identify objects, their attributes, and operations that participate in Use Cases.</li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Communication diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>reveal the structural requirements that are necessary to complete a task</li><li>explicitly identify the objects that participate in an interaction</li><li>reveal the interface requirements of the participating classes</li><li>identify the structural changes required to satisfy an interaction</li><li>identify data that passed as part of interactions. </li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="communication_diagram_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Communication diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f3253b09-6b76-40b6-ba80-9e4315f36012"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">307612155</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189139807 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00198: Communication diagram elements

- page_id: `189139807`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189139807/Communication+diagram+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:26.131+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Importing data from other UML tools and formats > Enterprise Architect Import > Special Transformation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

492179710

492179713

492179709

**On this page**

3

492179711

##### Object, Boundary, Entity, and Control

All Object, Boundary, Entity, Control elements in EA are [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='']. After conversion, their UML element types will remain, and a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Lifeline' space='']and an OwnedAttribute element will be created to represent each of them in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communication diagram' space=''] in which they occur.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Object, Boundary, Entity, and Control (diagram view).

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Object, Boundary, Entity, and Control (Containment tree).

Other elements that can be drawn in a Communication diagram will be handled in a similar manner.

##### Exception Elements

Some of the elements that can be drawn in a Communication diagram using EA are not supported by MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM. Consequently, their displaying parts will not be imported. These elements include [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram frame' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exception Handler' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Object Node' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Managing regions' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Object Node' space=''], Trigger, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expansion Region and Expansion Nodes' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Exception elements.

###### Realization and Nesting

The Realization element type in EA is *uml:Realization*. The Nesting element type in EA is exported in XMI as *uml:Dependency*. The Realization and Nesting lines in EA are not supported in the Communication diagram; therefore, only their model data will be imported, not the displaying parts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Realization and Nesting.

##### Association

Every Association relationship drawn in a Communication diagram in EA will have a Connector line created for each of them. The elements attached to both ends of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association End' space='']line will have a Lifeline element created to represent each of them. The Association lines and the elements attached to them will not be removed. However, the elements that will be shown in the diagram frame will be the Lifeline elements and the Connector lines created to represent them.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Association.

If an Association line is connected to the InstanceSpecification elements, its data will be removed. If an Association line is linked between two InstanceSpecification elements, it will be transformed into an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance Specification' space='']element. This is one of the constraints belonging to the Communication diagram.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Association Line between InstanceSpecifications.

##### Message

Messages can be created on Connectors and will be imported to MagicDraw, CEA, or CSM.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Message.

##### Operations

An InstanceSpecification cannot contain Operation elements. If the XMI file from EA has some InstanceSpecification elements containing Operation elements, those Operations will be removed.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Operations.

492185720

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Communication diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The Nesting element type in EA is exported in XMI as <em>uml:Dependency</em>. The Realization and Nesting lines in EA are not supported in the Communication diagram; therefore, only their model data will be imported, not the displaying parts.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="realization_nesting.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Realization and Nesting.</h6><h3>Association</h3><p>Every Association relationship drawn in a Communication diagram in EA will have a Connector line created for each of them. The Association lines and the elements attached to them will not be removed. However, the elements that will be shown in the diagram frame will be the Lifeline elements and the Connector lines created to represent them.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="association_beforeconversion.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="association_afterconversion.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Association.</h6><p>If an Association line is connected to the InstanceSpecification elements, its data will be removed. If an Association line is linked between two InstanceSpecification elements, it will be transformed into an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Instance Specification" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[InstanceSpecification ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>element. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141311 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00199: Communication Path

- page_id: `189141311`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141311/Communication+Path
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:05.465+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Association
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Communication path is a type of association between nodes in a deployment diagram that shows how the nodes exchange messages and signals.

When you specify communication paths, you can define the number of nodes that can be connected at each end. You can also use labels to identify the type of protocol or network that is used in the communication.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Communication path is a type of association between nodes in a deployment diagram that shows how the nodes exchange messages and signals.</p><p class="p">When you specify communication paths, you can define the number of nodes that can be connected at each end. You can also use labels to identify the type of protocol or network that is used in the communication.</p><p><br /></p>
````

## PAGE 00200: Comparing projects

- page_id: `189143193`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189143193/Comparing+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:39:46.249+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis
- labels: ['base', 'compared', 'compare-concepts']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

641794038

641794050

641794040

The Compare Projects functionality allows you to compare:

- Two server project versions
- Open server project with a local or server project version
- Open a local project with a server project version

Various different properties of the elements that share the same ID in different projects are compared.

##### Basic concepts

For a better understanding of the further material, learn the following concepts.

| Concept | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Base | The original project version without changes. |
| Compared | A project version that contains modifications added to the original project version. |

641794035

**Related pages**

2

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141089 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00201: Compartments

- page_id: `189141089`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141089/Compartments
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:48.530+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The compartment is an area on the shape or next to the shape that is dedicated to the specific type of information.

There are the following types of compartments:

- Compartment boxes. For example, the class shape has compartments that put attributes, operations, and signals into boxes. [ATTACHMENT filename='compartments.png']

- You can hide compartments by suppressing them or expand the hidden compartments to see their data.
- Compartment names are displayed by default. However, you can hide them by changing their visibility settings either in the Symbol Properties dialog or the Project Options dialog. Each compartment has its own property for visibility settings (for example, Show Attributes Compartment Name ).

- Compartments that are displayed next to the element name on the shape. For example, a shape has compartments for stereotypes, constraints, and others.

- Stereotypes (or stereotypes compartment) are displayed above the element name.
- Constraints (or constraints compartment) are displayed under the element name.
- By default, tagged values are displayed under the element name, or you can select to display tagged values in the compartment box.
- Element Properties are displayed in the compartment box.

- Compartments that are displayed next to the element name, next to the shape. For example, an Actor has a compartment for element properties, a compartment for stereotypes, behaviors, and others.
- Compartments on paths that are displayed next to the path label. For example, a compartment for element properties, a compartment for stereotypes, and others.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Path compartments' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

However, you can hide them by changing their visibility settings either in the <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog or the <strong>Project Options</strong> dialog. Each compartment has its own property for visibility settings (for example,<strong> Show Attributes Compartment Name</strong>).</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><span>Compartments that are displayed next to the element name on the shape. For example, a </span>shape has compartments for stereotypes, constraints, and others.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="75431075-9e9b-40a2-ba94-8537daa1914a"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Stereotypes (or stereotypes compartment) are displayed above the element name.</li><li>Constraints (or constraints compartment) are displayed under the element name.</li><li>By default, tagged values are displayed under the element name, or you can select to display tagged values in the compartment box.</li><li>Element Properties are displayed in the compartment box.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ul><li><span>Compartments that are displayed next to the element name, next to the shape. For example, an Actor has a compartment for element properties, a compartment for stereotypes, behaviors, and others.</span><span><br class="_mce_tagged_br" /></span></li><li><span>Compartments on paths that are displayed next to the path label. For example, a compartment for element properties, a compartment for stereotypes, and others.</span></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5727cdfa-2292-4697-9b06-459e6f9fccdf" /></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Diagramming" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Path compartments" /></ac:link></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141214 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00202: Complete diagrams

- page_id: `189141214`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141214/Complete+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:59.306+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Updating multiple paths

31649184

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Updating multiple paths

31649186

31649182

**On this page**

4

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Updating multiple paths

31649185

##### Marking diagrams as complete

When you finish working on a diagram, you expect that the diagram layout will not change. However, if the content of your model is changed, the diagram layout changes as well; shapes are automatically resized to fit new content and labels are automatically displayed on paths. This can cause symbols to overlap and paths to be left hanging in a diagram, as illustrated below. So you must go back to the finished diagram and fix its layout.To save time, you can mark diagrams as complete andprevent their layout from being automatically updated.

700500

To mark a diagram as complete

- Right-click the diagram on the diagram pane or in the model browser and from the menu select Complete .

When a diagram is marked as complete, the following happens:

- The «complete» stereotype is applied to a diagram. This way you can easily indicate complete diagrams in the model browser without opening them.
- Auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels is suspended. This means that the diagram layout is not automatically changed when the model content changes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The figure displays an example of a complete diagram.

#### NOTE: Alternative

Alternative

You can suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in a diagram without marking it as complete in one of the following ways:

- To suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels for individual symbols, o pen the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog of a symbol or a group of symbols, and set the Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes and/or Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels properties to true .
- To suspend auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels for all the diagram symbols, o pen the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying diagram style properties' space='']**Diagram Properties** dialog of a diagram, and set the Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes and/or Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels properties to true.

##### Updating complete diagram layout

When a diagram is marked as complete, the symbols with suspended content related to model changes are highlighted in blue (see the figure above). You can update these symbols manually, as described below.

To update shapes

1. In a complete diagram, select the highlighted shape you want to update.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Drag one of the selection handles on the corner of the shape to resize it. The suspended content is dynamically displayed after resizing the shape.
  - Click the smart manipulator on the bottom of the shape, as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='updating_shape.png']
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='solver.png'] on the shape toolbar and select Resize to Fit Content .

#### TIP: Updating multiple shapes

Updating multiple shapes

To update multiple shapes in a complete diagram, select the highlighted shapes you want to update and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in the diagram toolbar.

To update paths

1. In a complete diagram, select the highlighted path you want to update.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Click the smart manipulator on the path, as shown below. [ATTACHMENT filename='updating_path.png']
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='solver.png'] on the path toolbar and select Show Suspended Labels .

#### TIP: Updating multiple paths

Updating multiple paths

To update multiple paths in a complete diagram, select the highlighted paths you want to update and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] in the diagram toolbar.

##### Marking diagrams as incomplete

If you need to resume normal work on a diagram, mark it as incomplete, as described below.

To mark a diagram as incomplete

1. Right-click a diagram on the diagram pane or in the model browser and select the Complete option to clear its check box.
2. When the question dialog is displayed, do one of the following:
  - Select **Yes**, to continue suspending auto-resizing of shapes and/or auto-displaying of labels.
  - Select No , to resume auto-resizing of shapes and/or auto-displaying of labels.

If you select**No**in step 2, all diagram symbols are automatically resized to fit suspended content, andyou can continue working on the diagram as usual. If you select **Yes**, auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in the diagram are still suspended, even though the diagram is not complete.

##### Customizing complete diagram behavior

By default, auto-resizing of shapes and auto-displaying of labels in a complete diagram are suspended. Also, the symbols with suspended content due to model changes are highlighted in blue. You can change this default behavior in the **Project Options** dialog.

To customize complete diagram behavior

1. In the main menu of your modeling tool, go to Options > Project .
2. On the left side of the Project Options dialog, expand the General node and select Diagrams .
3. On the right side of the dialog, specify the desired options in the Complete Diagrams group:
  - Suspend Auto-Resizing of Shapes - set to true to keep the original size of shapes while editing the model.
  - Suspend Auto-Displaying of Labels - set to true if you do not want to automatically show labels in a diagram while editing the model.
  - Highlight Suspended Symbols - set to true to highlight symbols that have suspended content related to model changes.

When you specify project options listed in step 3, they are applied to all diagrams that are marked as complete after specification.

##### **Check complete diagrams**

To check complete diagrams

- In the main menu of your modeling tool, select Analyze > Check Complete Diagrams .

The **Check Complete Diagrams**action checks for complete diagrams without completely visible content by running the following three validation rules:

| Validation rule | Error message | Severity | Profile |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Suspended Autodisplaying | The symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-displaying of labels is suspended. | warning | UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile |
| Suspended Autoresizing | The symbol content is not completely visible. Auto-resizing of shapes is suspended. | warning | UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile |
| Suspended Autochanges | The diagram contains symbols whose content is not completely visible. | warning | UML Standard Profile::Validation Profile |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141377 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00203: Component

- page_id: `189141377`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141377/Component
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:08.494+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Component represents all kinds of elements that make up the system. A Component can always be considered as an autonomous unit within a system or subsystem. It is a module having the following features:

- A Component is a replaceable and independent part of the system performing a specific action.
- A Component acts in a context of a well-defined architecture.
- Components interact among each other using [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space=''] .

Components provides compartments for listing its provided and required Interfaces, realizations, and artifacts.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

A Component can be specified by changing its property values in the Component [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']. Each property is described in the description area on this window.

To show/hide the interfaces, realizations, and artifacts on the Component’s shape, do one of the following

- On the diagram, right-click the Component’s shape and then from the shortcut menu, select Symbol Properties . In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog , set the Suppress Interfaces , Suppress Realizations , or Suppress Artifacts property values to true or false .
- On the diagram, click the Component’s shape and then click the Compartments button [ATTACHMENT filename='compartments.png'] . Then in the menu, click to select or clear the Interfaces , Realizations , or Artifacts check box.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>A Component represents all kinds of elements that make up the system. A Component can always be considered as an autonomous unit within a system or subsystem. It is a module having the following features:</p><ul><li>A Component is a replaceable and independent part of the system performing a specific action.</li><li>A Component acts in a context of a well-defined architecture.</li><li>Components interact among each other using <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</li></ul><p>Components provides compartments for listing its provided and required Interfaces, realizations, and artifacts.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="An example of a Component" ac:alt="An example of a Component"><ri:attachment ri:filename="component_listed_properties.png" /></ac:image></p><p>A Component can be specified by changing its property values in the Component <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link>. Each property is described in the description area on this window.</p><p> </p><p>To show/hide the interfaces, realizations, and artifacts on the Component’s shape, do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>On the diagram, right-click the Component’s shape and then from the shortcut menu, select <strong>Symbol Properties</strong>. In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /><ac:link-body> <strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>, set the <strong>Suppress Interfaces</strong>, <strong>Suppress Realizations</strong>, or <strong>Suppress Artifacts</strong> property values to <em>true</em> or <em> false</em>.</li><li>On the diagram, click the Component’s shape and then click the Compartments button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="compartments.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image>. Then in the menu, click to select or clear the <strong>Interfaces</strong>, <strong>Realizations</strong>, or <strong>Artifacts</strong> check box.</li></ul></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b2025c5-70c1-47b6-9a1b-4fb242548002"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Class diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Class diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Component diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140225 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00204: Component diagram

- page_id: `189140225`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140225/Component+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:47.053+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: ['implementation', 'implementation-diagram']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308582438

308582440

308582439

**Overview**

A Component diagram falls under the structural diagramming family.A Component diagram represents pieces of software in the implementation environment in terms of code components.

A [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space=''] contains information about the logical [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space=''] or Classes that it implements, thus creating a mapping from a logical view to a component view. Dependencies between the Components make it easy to analyze how a change in one component affects the others. The Components may also be shown with any of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interface' space=''] that they expose. They, as with almost any other model elements, can be grouped into [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space=''], much like Classes or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case' space=''].

**Purpose**

A Component diagram models the implementation view. It is used in the later phases of the software development when there is a need to divide up Classes among different Components. When working with the CASE facilities, the Components are used for file-class mapping during code generation, reverse engineering, and round-trip engineering operations.****

**Usage**

A Component diagram can be used to:

- analyze system components and how they relate
- analyze system's physical structure
- understand the exact service behavior provided by software.

**Summary**

Component diagrams are valuable because they:

- reveal possible software configuration issues
- provide an accurate picture of existing systems prior to making changes/enhancements
- model the real software in the implementation environment
- help to reveal bottlenecks in an implementation.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Component diagram

308582437

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="267db836-c4c9-404c-bf3c-b4ec48757657"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308582438</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="04fd79d1-7e3c-4b8a-8180-18696ef1f6f2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308582440</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9bc4285f-5a74-4187-9f70-d5955f4ffb00"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308582439</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Overview</strong></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">A Component diagram falls under the structural diagramming family. </span>A Component diagram represents pieces of software in the implementation environment in terms of code components. </p><p>A <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Component" /></ac:link> contains information about the logical <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Class" /></ac:link> or Classes that it implements, thus creating a mapping from a logical view to a component view. Dependencies between the Components make it easy to analyze how a change in one component affects the others. The Components may also be shown with any of the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Interfaces]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> that they expose. They, as with almost any other model elements, can be grouped into <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Package" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Packages]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, much like Classes or <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Use Cases]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><strong>Purpose</strong></p><p>A Component diagram models the implementation view. It is used in the later phases of the software development when there is a need to divide up Classes among different Components. When working with the CASE facilities, the Components are used for file-class mapping during code generation, reverse engineering, and round-trip engineering operations.<strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>A Component diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>analyze system components and how they relate</li><li>analyze system's physical structure</li><li>understand the exact service behavior provided by software. </li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Component diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>reveal possible software configuration issues</li><li>provide an accurate picture of existing systems prior to making changes/enhancements</li><li>model the real software in the implementation environment</li><li>help to reveal bottlenecks in an implementation. <br /><br /></li></ul><p class="auto-cursor-target"><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="component_diagram_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Component diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08d36afe-7d48-42c1-b72e-10091cb4c272"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308582437</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

## PAGE 00205: Component realization

- page_id: `189141379`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141379/Component+realization
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:08.688+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Component Realization concept is specified in the Components package to (optionally) define the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying the classifier' space='']. The Classifier defines the contract offered by a component in terms of its provided and required interfaces. The component forms an abstraction from these various Classifiers.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">A Component Realization concept is specified in the Components package to (optionally) define the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specifying the classifier" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Classifier]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. The Classifier defines the contract offered by a component in terms of its provided and required interfaces. The component forms an abstraction from these various Classifiers.</span></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140243 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00207: Composite Structure diagram

- page_id: `189140243`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140243/Composite+Structure+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:47.894+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

308768822

308768824

308768823

**Overview**

A Composite Structure diagram falls under the structural diagramming family.The Composite Structure diagram allows a decomposition and modeling of the internal structure of the classifiers, such as [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Component' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Node' space=''], and other elements that are able to own other elements.

**Purpose**

A Composite Structure diagram can be used to visually represent the parts of a class, component, or collaboration, including the interaction points (called ports) that are used to access features of the structure. Additionally, Composite Structure diagrams model collaborations that describe behavior, resources used to perform the behavior, and the roles the participating resources take during the behavior.

**Usage**

Composite Structure diagram can be used to:

- analyze an object to see exactly what it is composed of
- detail internal actions of a class, including the relationships of nested classes
- show objects that need to be defined as a composition of other classified objects.

**Summary**

Composite Structure diagrams are valuable because they:

- show complex components design
- show components interfaces separately from their structures
- describe the roles played by elements in the structure.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of a Composite Structure diagram

308768820

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

Additionally, Composite Structure diagrams model collaborations that describe behavior, resources used to perform the behavior, and the roles the participating resources take during the behavior. </p><p><strong>Usage</strong></p><p>Composite Structure diagram can be used to:</p><ul><li>analyze an object to see exactly what it is composed of</li><li>detail internal actions of a class, including the relationships of nested classes</li><li>show objects that need to be defined as a composition of other classified objects.</li></ul><p><strong>Summary</strong></p><p>Composite Structure diagrams are valuable because they:</p><ul><li>show complex components design</li><li>show components interfaces separately from their structures</li><li>describe the roles played by elements in the structure.</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="composite_structure_diagram_example.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of a Composite Structure diagram</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e03f3fe1-a2a8-47af-9d4e-3024c4e6339a"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">308768820</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating diagrams" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="42f4595c-1db3-4b0e-a549-bdd8f01b65b3" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140245 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00208: Composite Structure Diagram context

- page_id: `189140245`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140245/Composite+Structure+Diagram+context
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:45:17.635+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > UML diagrams > Composite Structure diagram
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When creating a new [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Composite Structure diagram' space=''] for the element that cannot be the context of this diagram (e.g. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='']), the new context element (which is a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='']) is created automatically.

INLINE

##### Turning off automatic creation of a diagram context element

To turn off automatic creation of a diagram context element

1. On the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] , select Project > Options .
2. In the Project Options dialog, find the Diagram Context option.
3. Select the **Do Not Create** value.
4. Click OK . The automatic creation of a diagram context element is turned off.

This means that when youcreate a newdiagramforan element which cannot be the context of this diagram (e.g. [Package](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Package)),the**Select Diagram Context**dialog will open automatically.It allows you to select an existing element as a context, create a new context element by enabling the**Creation Mode**, or click the**Cancel**button if you do not want to specify the diagram context. The diagram will be created in the Package.

If you want to enable the automatic creation of a diagram context element, select the **Create Automatically** value for the **Diagram Context** option.

##### Changing the diagram context element

After the diagram is created, you can change its context element.

To change the diagram context element

1. Right-click the diagram and select Specification .
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']Specification window , find the Context property.
3. Click on its value cell, and select [ATTACHMENT filename='edit_button_in_specification_window.png'] .
4. In the **Select Diagram Context** dialog, do one of the following: 
 - Select an existing element as a context in the **Tree** or **List** tab 
 - Create a new context element by enabling the **Creation Mode**>**Create**and select it.
5. Click **OK**. The diagram context element is changed.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

