The constraint assigned to the Constraint Link is labeled in braces: {} . See an example of the constraint representation in the following figure.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Constraint Link between relationships" ac:alt="Constraint Link between relationships"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraint_link.png" /></ac:image></p><p>On the Constraint Link, you can display the constraint direction arrow. The direction of the arrow represents a relevant information within the constraint. The client (the tail of the arrow) is mapped to the first position and the supplier (arrowhead) is mapped to the second position in the constraint.</p><p>You can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Formatting symbols" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[format ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>the constraint symbol properties in the  <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Symbol Properties dialog" /></ac:link>.</p><p>After applying the constraint to the Constraint Link, you can <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Editing property values" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[specify ]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>the constraint in the Constraint specification window. See an example in the following figure.</p></li></ol><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of constraint path with direction arrow" ac:alt="Example of constraint path with direction arrow"><ri:attachment ri:filename="constraint_path_arrow.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p> </p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b2025c5-70c1-47b6-9a1b-4fb242548002"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related Pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Class diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Class diagrams]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Actions" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Applying duration constraint on the Action]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141391 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00222: Containment

- page_id: `189141391`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141391/Containment
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:45:25.411+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

943409377

943409379

943409378

The Containment relationship represents the containment of elements on the diagram pane. A Containment path replaces a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Nesting Requirements' space='SYSMLP185']. For example, instead of drawing one package nested into another, you can draw a containment relationship from one package to another (see the figure below).This example shows that the Containment relationship is a graphical representation as well as a nesting of elements.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of package nesting (on the left) and package nesting using containment relationship (on the right)

In the example above, the Containment end with a circle plus symbol shows a containing element. The *User* package is a contained element and the*Domain*package is a containing element: the owner of the*User*package is the*Domain*package.

You can format containment symbol properties in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='']**Symbol Properties** dialog.

943409376

**Related pages**

- Generating reports from the Containment tree
- Customizing Environment Options
- Class Diagram
- Composite Structure Diagram
- Use Case Diagram
- Component Diagram
- Object Diagram
- Package Diagram
- Model Elements

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0f9f86af-b8b9-4a4a-99c2-038a6a21c270"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">943409377</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="62dcc777-4459-4da2-ad37-df8f08b74331"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">943409379</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="cbcf2e95-266c-4773-8fc2-ab2f7a419bf3"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">943409378</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">The Containment relationship represents the containment of elements on the diagram pane. A Containment path replaces a <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP185" ri:content-title="Nesting Requirements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Nesting representation]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. For example, instead of drawing one package nested into another, you can draw a containment relationship from one package to another (see the figure below). </span><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">This example shows that the Containment relationship is a graphical representation as well as a nesting of elements. </span></p><h6><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="containment.png" /></ac:image></h6><h6 style="text-align: center;"><span>Example of package nesting (on the left) and package nesting using containment relationship (on the right) </span></h6><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 14.0px;">In the example above, the Containment end with a circle plus symbol shows a containing element. The</span> <em>User</em> <span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 14.0px;">package is a contained element and the </span><em>Domain</em><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 14.0px;"> package is a containing element: the owner of the </span><em>User</em><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 14.0px;"> package is the </span><em>Domain</em><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);font-size: 14.0px;"> package.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">You can format containment symbol properties in the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Formatting symbols" /><ac:link-body><strong>Symbol Properties</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link><span style="color: rgb(0,0,0);">. </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="db4b8067-427d-489d-9b29-9f7f5e88e765"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">943409376</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro"><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Generating+reports+from+the+Containment+tree">Generating reports from the Containment tree</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Customizing+environment+options">Customizing Environment Options</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Class">Class Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Composite+Structure+diagram">Composite Structure Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Use+Case+diagram">Use Case Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Component+diagram">Component Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Object+diagram">Object Diagram</a></li><li><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Package+diagram">Package Diagram</a></li><li class="ancestor-link parent-link"><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Model+elements">Model Elements</a></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189139575 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00223: Containment tab

- page_id: `189139575`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189139575/Containment+tab
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:11.753+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Getting started > Understanding the user interface > Model Browser
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

469061243

469061246

469098799

**On this page**

469061245

INLINE

The Containment tab displays model data grouped in logical sets. Using the Containment tab, you can quickly access each model element, change the model element's specification, or create a new model element.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To open the Containment tab, do one of the following

- At the top of the Model Browser, click the Containment tab.
- If the Containment tab is hidden, from the **Window** menu, select **Containment**. You can change some Containment tab properties in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space='']**Environment Options** dialog in the **Browser** options group.

The Containment tab consists of the following two components:

- 
- 

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The Containment tree includes

- The root package *Model*:

The Relations branch 
The UML Standard Profile 
The Hyperlinks branch

- 

##### #datamodelThe root package *Model*

The root package*Model* in the Containment tree is the top model element wherein the entire model structure is created. The root package***Model* represents the model element that is named *Model*. All model elements are stored in the root package***Model*. You can create the structure of your project by creating packages in the root package *Model*. This helps to distribute model elements into logical groups.

Along with model elements that are created by the user, the root package *Model* may contain the following default items:

- The Relations branch. This branch appears when at least one relationship is created in the project. The Relations branch collects all relationships from the particular owner. For example, if the Relations branch is in the root package Model , that means, all relationships from the root package Model are listed in the Relations branch owned by the root package Model . The Relations branch can exist in other elements which may have inner elements and relations among these elements. For example, in packages, classes, and others.
- The **UML Standard Profile** package. This is a profile that is included in every project. It contains a list of stereotypes, data types, and other elements from the UML 2 metamodel. By default, the *UML Standard Profile* is hidden. To display the *UML Standard Profile*, click the Options button, and in the shortcut menu, choose**Show****Auxiliary****Resources**. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']. 
There can be more standard profiles (such as SysML, UPDM, Java, and others). We highly recommend not to modify our provided standard profiles and libraries as it could cause problems with version updates, plugins, core modeling tool malfunctions, and model corruptions.
- The File View package. This package appears when at least one code engineering set is created. The File View package contains components that are created on code reverse and represents source files.
- The Hyperlinks branch. This branch appears when at least one hyperlink is added to a particular element. The Hyperlinks branch contains the list of hyperlinks to the file, element/ symbol, or web page.

##### #codeengineeringsetsCode engineering sets

Code engineering is available in Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

Code engineering sets can be considered a gateway between your source code and model data. Using these sets, you can perform Java, C++, IDL, CORBA IDL, and EJB 2.0 round-trip code engineering, that is, code generation and reverse engineering. 
 
To display Code engineering sets

- In the Containment tab toolbar, click the Options [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button, then click to select the Show Code Engineering Sets check box.

In the Code engineering Set branch, you can create a new code engineering set, generate code, and perform other actions. 
****

##### Containment tab toolbar

#containmenttoolbar

The Containment tab toolbar contains the following buttons:

| Button | Tooltip text | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Collapse All | Collapses all of the currently expanded elements in the Containment tree. |
|  | Collapse Selected Recursively | Collapses only selected node and all subnodes in that node. |
|  | Open in New Tree | Opens the contents of the selected element in a new tab of the Model Browser. The selected element becomes the root node of the new tree.You can choose to open elements in:New Containment TreeNew Structure TreeNew Inheritance Tree |
|  | Favorites | Opens the Favorites menu for choosing to do one of the following:Add a selected element to favoritesOpen the dialog for managing favorites Navigate to a desired favorite in the Containment tree |
|  | Quick Find | Opens the Quick Find dialog. |
|  | Options | Click to open the Options menu and then select:Filter - opens the Select Element Type dialog wherein you can select element types to be visible in the Containment tree. Learn more on the page.Show Auxiliary Resources - shows or hides modules and profiles that are used in your project.Show Applied Stereotypes - shows or hides the applied stereotypes next to the element name in the Containment tree.Show Full Types - shows or hides the full types of typed elements (for example, operations, attributes, relationships, and other) in the Containment tree.Show Code Engineering Sets - shows or hides the code engineering sets. |

The same actions you may perform using the shortcut menu of the Containment tree.

##### Working with model elements in the Containment tree

#containmenttree

You can work with elements in the Containment tree using the commands available from the selected element's shortcut menu.

Particular actions are available for particular elements.

The shortcut menu contains the following commands:

| Command | Function |
| --- | --- |
|  | Creates a new element inside the selected element.This command is available if the selected element can be edited. |
|  | Creates a new diagram inside the selected element.This command is available if the selected element can be edited. |
| Create Relation > Outgoing / Incoming | Creates a new relation to or from the selected element. |
|  | Opens the element Specification window wherein you can specify various element properties. |
|  | Open the validation commands for checking the accuracy, completeness, and correctness of a model. |
|  | Opens or selects the item associated with the selected element. The item may be a diagram, model element, or hyperlink. |
|  | Converts the selected element to the element indicated by a user. |
|  | Opens a list of functions available for the selected element. |
| Tools | Opens a list of tools that are available for the selected element. Depends on the selected element. |
|  | Opens the drop-down list, wherein you can apply an existing stereotype, or create a new one. |
|  | Opens the Find dialog. |
| Apply Profiles | Opens the list of the available to apply profiles.This command is available in the package, model, and system boundary shortcut menu.Only custom profiles can be applied.To see this command you have to expand the shortcut menu, that is, click on the little arrow at the bottom of the shortcut menu. |
|  | Opens share packages and export packages to new project commands.This command is available in the package and model shortcut menu. |
| Generate Code Framework | Generates code for the selected item. Opens the Notification Window with the information. |
| Check Syntax | Checks syntax in the model according to the default code engineering language. Opens the Notification Window with the information. |
|  | Opens the list of the available report templates. |
| Reverse from Classpath | This command is available in the Class shortcut menu. |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141394 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00224: Containment Tree

- page_id: `189141394`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141394/Containment+Tree
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:10.048+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Containment
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

943711221

943711224

943711223

You can create the Containment tree when drawing more than one Containment relationship.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Example of several containment relationships (on the left) and example of containment tree (on the right)

To group containment paths into a tree

1. Draw a Containment path from each contained element to the containing element.
2. Now, do one of the following:

- Through the diagram pane.
  1. Select the Containment relationship.
  2. Click and hold the little square (the end of the Containment path) next to the Containment end with the circle plus symbol.
  3. Drag and drop the selected Containment end on the other Containment path.
  4. While dragging, the Containment path turns blue, indicating an allowable connection. Containment paths merge together. [ATTACHMENT filename='DnD_containment_path.png']

- Through the shortcut menu.
  1. Open the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Shortcut menu' space='CDH185'] of the containing element, in the example above: Domain .
  2. Click Refactor > Make Sub Tree .

To ungroup a containment tree

1. Right-click the Containment tree's end with the circle plus symbol pointing to a containing element.
2. In the opened shortcut menu, click the Ungroup Tree command. [ATTACHMENT filename='ungroup_containment_tree.png']

To separate the containment path from the containment tree

- Select the containment path you want to remove from the tree. Open its shortcut menu and click the **Remove From Tree** command. The contained element is added to the containing element separately. [ATTACHMENT filename='remove_containment_from_tree.png']
- Drag one Containment path to another element:
  1. On the diagram pane, select the vertical part of the particular Containment path.
  2. Click and hold the upper little square.
  3. Drag and drop end of the Containment path to the other containing element.
  4. The Containment path is removed from the Containment tree and the owner of the containing element has been changed.

943711219

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generating reports from the Containment tree' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space='']

## PAGE 00225: Content diagram

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Functionality Availability

23

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Functionality Availability

24

INLINE

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Functionality Availability

26

INLINE

#### NOTE: Functionality Availability

Functionality Availability

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

The Content diagram is an extension of UML notation. The purpose of the Content diagram is to generate or represent a project structure (diagrams) and the relations between them. The Content table works as a table of contents for a project. The Content Shape creates a table of contents of all diagrams of the project.

All Content diagrams have their own specifications. You can specify their name, documentation, and view the relationships in which they participate. You can also add [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space=''], [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Editing tagged value' space=''], and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Constraint' space='']. For more information about diagram creation, read [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space=''].

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Content diagram palette

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example a Content diagram for the Free Form diagram

27

INLINE

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extension diagrams' space='']

## PAGE 00226: Content Diagram Wizard

- page_id: `189143424`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

638104837

638104840

638104838

This feature is available in Standard, Professional, Architect, and Enterprise editions.

The **Content Diagram Wizard** generates the content of diagrams used in the project.

To create a content diagram using the **Content Diagram Wizard**

1. Open the Content Diagram Wizard by doing one of the following:
  - On the Diagrams menu, select Diagram Wizards > Content Diagram Wizard .
  - From the Analyze menu, select Model Visualizer . In the open Model Visualizer dialog, select to open the Content Diagram Wizard and click **Start** .
2. Follow the steps as directed by the wizard.

638104835

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Content diagram' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141402 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00227: Control Flow

- page_id: `189141402`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141402/Control+Flow
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:45:25.569+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A Control flow is an edge that starts an [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Action' space=''] node after the previous one is finished.

You can specify a Control flow by changing its property values in the Control flow[Specification window](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Specification+window). Each property is described in the description area on this window.

You can format the Control flow symbol properties in the[Symbol Properties dialog](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Formatting+symbols).

A Control flow can have guards (constraints that provide fine-grained control over the firing of the Control Flow). To specify guards, choose the Guard box in the Control Flow dialog box.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Specification of the Control Flow, Guard property

Set the Control Flow symbol properties in the Symbol Properties dialog.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>A Control flow is an edge that starts an <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Action" /></ac:link> node after the previous one is finished.</p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You can specify a Control flow by changing its property values in the Control flow </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Specification+window">Specification window</a><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. Each property is described in the description area on this window.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">You can format the Control flow symbol properties in the </span><a href="https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Formatting+symbols">Symbol Properties dialog</a><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">.</span><br /></span></p><p>A Control flow can have guards (constraints that provide fine-grained control over the firing of the Control Flow). <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140000 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00228: Controlling dependency creation between used projects

- page_id: `189140000`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140000/Controlling+dependency+creation+between+used+projects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:41.127+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Project partitioning > Automated project usages
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In earlier versions of our modeling tool, you could accidentally create unneeded or incorrect (from the user standpoint) dependencies between used projects in the read/write mode. Careless editing often caused cyclic module dependencies or inadvertent expansion of the scope of the other projects when the same used project was used in several other projects.

Automated project usages fix this problem.

Every automatically created usage must be backed by an existing user-defined used project usage. If it is not backed, the automated used project usage must be explicitly confirmed by the user. The usage cannot cause undesired effects in other projects while it is unconfirmed.

Automated used project usage *A**→**B* is called**unconfirmed**when there is no user-defined usage path from A to B (neither direct user-defined usage*A**→**B* nor user-defined usage path of arbitrary length *A**→ ... → X →**B*).

User-defined usages are always confirmed.

In ordinary circumstances, automated usages are not visible to the user. When the user modifies the main project’s model and adds dependencies to used projects or sub-used projects, these usages are proper. They are backed by the fact that the used project or sub-used project is attached to the project in some way; thus, the user-defined usage path refers from the main project to the used project or sub-used project.

However, there are situations when automated used project usages are not backed and thus unconfirmed.

An unconfirmed automated used project usage is considered to be invalid.

This is just one of the project composition errors. Other project composition errors include similarity to recovered element errors, conflicting user-defined usage parameters, and cyclic dependencies.

Unconfirmed automated used project usages are caught by special [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Active Validation Rules for Links' space='']. They are displayed in validation results as warnings or errors depending on the project composition. The validation results prompt the user to remedy the situation by either confirming or rejecting the usage.

Unconfirmed usages can occur in several cases. They are described as follows.

#### INFO: Case #1

Case #1

When you edit a model part belonging to a used project with read/write permissions, an additional model-level reference can be created either back to the project itself (cycle) or to another used project.

These model-level usages may or may not be valid from the user point of view. Since the creation of usages is very easy, you can inadvertently create undesired usages. On the other hand, usages can be entirely benign. Our modeling tool cannot decide whether the usage is good or bad. For example, creating references from the used project *Implementation* to the used project *Requirements* is valid, while creating references in the opposite direction may be invalid since requirements usually are standalone, and implementation refers to them.

#### INFO: Case #2

Case #2

Reorganization or removal of sub-used projects may affect the projects or other used projects using the used project. This happens only in cases when a new refactored used project does not carry all of the contents that the old used project contained.

Consider a used project *ElectricComponents* with three sub-used projects: *Capacitors, Inductances,* and *Resistances.* Also consider a project *RadioSet,* which uses the used project *ElectricComponents.*Now let’s use one model element from each sub-used project in the project *RadioSet.* As a result, the three automated usages

*RadioSet* → *Capacitors,*

*RadioSet* → *Inductances,*and**

*RadioSet* → *Resistances*

will be created.

If the used project *ElectricComponents* was refactored by re-importing the sub-used project *Capacitors* and *Inductances* and completely separating the sub-used project *Resistances,* then when loading the project *RadioSet,* the automated usages *RadioSet* → *Capacitors* and *RadioSet* → *Inductances* will be automatically updated. However, the usage *RadioSet* → *Resistors* will remain unaddressed. It will appear as an unconfirmed used project usage, and you will have to confirm it, i.e., explicitly attach the used project *Resistances* to the project by creating the user-defined usage.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span>In earlier versions of our modeling tool, you could accidentally create unneeded or incorrect (from the user standpoint) dependencies between used projects in the read/write mode. Careless editing often caused cyclic module dependencies or inadvertent expansion of the scope of the other projects when the same used project was used in several other projects.</span></p><p><span style="text-align: center;">Automated project usages fix this problem.</span></p><p><span>Every automatically created usage must be backed by an existing user-defined used project usage. If it is not backed, the automated used project usage must be explicitly confirmed by the user. The usage cannot cause undesired effects in other projects while it is unconfirmed. </span></p><p><span>Automated used project usage <em>A </em></span><em>→ </em><span><em>B</em> is called </span><span><strong>unconfirmed</strong> </span><span>when there is no user-defined usage path from A to B (neither direct user-defined usage<em> A </em></span><em>→ </em><span><em>B</em> nor user-defined usage path of arbitrary length <em>A </em></span><em>→ ... → X → </em><span><em>B</em>). </span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="87a6ff2e-53c4-4353-beee-84af03df8344"><ac:rich-text-body>User-defined usages are always confirmed.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><p>In ordinary circumstances, automated usages are not visible to the user. When the user modifies the main project’s model and adds dependencies to used projects or sub-used projects, these usages are proper. They are backed by the fact that the used project or sub-used project is attached to the project in some way; thus, the user-defined usage path refers from the main project to the used project or sub-used project.</p><p>However, there are situations when automated used project usages are not backed and thus unconfirmed.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="808f52e0-54e8-48ad-97c6-d303a33d4766"><ac:rich-text-body>An unconfirmed automated used project usage is considered to be invalid.</ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>This is just one of the project composition errors. Other project composition errors include similarity to recovered element errors, conflicting user-defined usage parameters, and cyclic dependencies.</p><p>Unconfirmed automated used project usages are caught by special <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Active Validation Rules for Links" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[automatic validation rules]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>. They are displayed in validation results as warnings or errors depending on the project composition. The validation results prompt the user to remedy the situation by either confirming or rejecting the usage.</p><p>Unconfirmed usages can occur in several cases. They are described as follows.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2f54675e-1ffa-442e-9bb7-c0df1efb9642"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Case #1</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>When you edit a model part belonging to a used project with read/write permissions, an additional model-level reference can be created either back to the project itself (cycle) or to another used project.</p><p>These model-level usages may or may not be valid from the user point of view. Since the creation of usages is very easy, you can inadvertently create undesired usages. On the other hand, usages can be entirely benign. Our modeling tool cannot decide whether the usage is good or bad. For example, creating references from the used project <em>Implementation</em> to the used project <em>Requirements</em> is valid, while creating references in the opposite direction may be invalid since requirements usually are standalone, and implementation refers to them.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2a59384f-809c-41ea-b15f-791023c1e3f8"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Case #2</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>Reorganization or removal of sub-used projects may affect the projects or other used projects using the used project. This happens only in cases when a new refactored used project does not carry all of the contents that the old used project contained.</p><p>Consider a used project <em>ElectricComponents</em> with three sub-used projects: <em>Capacitors, Inductances,</em> and <em>Resistances.</em> Also consider a project <em>RadioSet,</em> which uses the used project <em>ElectricComponents. </em>Now let’s use one model element from each sub-used project in the project <em>RadioSet.</em> As a result, the three automated usages</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><em>RadioSet</em> → <em>Capacitors,</em></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><em>RadioSet</em> → <em>Inductances, </em>and<em> </em></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><em>RadioSet</em> → <em>Resistances</em></p><p>will be created.</p><p>If the used project <em>ElectricComponents</em> was refactored by re-importing the sub-used project <em>Capacitors</em> and <em>Inductances</em> and completely separating the sub-used project <em>Resistances,</em> then when loading the project <em>RadioSet,</em> the automated usages <em>RadioSet</em> → <em>Capacitors</em> and <em>RadioSet</em> → <em>Inductances</em> will be automatically updated. However, the usage <em>RadioSet</em> → <em>Resistors</em> will remain unaddressed. It will appear as an unconfirmed used project usage, and you will have to confirm it, i.e., explicitly attach the used project <em>Resistances</em> to the project by creating the user-defined usage.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142469 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00229: Controlling direction

- page_id: `189142469`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142469/Controlling+direction
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:39:16.080+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Transformation Wizard > Controlling Type Mapping Rule Behavior
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

By default, the same type map can be applied in two directions: forward and backward. The backward direction can be set by selecting the **Run type mapping in reverse order** check box in the third step of the Model Transformation Wizard. This is useful, when there are two related opposite transformations for some domain; for example, the same type map is used for both UML to XML schema and XML schema to UML transformations.

If you want to limit the directions, in which type map can be used, you can set the**defaultDirection** tag for your type map package. Possible values are **forward**, **reverse**, and **both** (default).

The direction can also be limited on a per-rule basis. This is controlled by setting the **direction** tag on the type map dependency. Then the mapping rule is excluded from the rule set when the type map is run in a different direction than specified for this rule.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>By default, the same type map can be applied in two directions: forward and backward. The backward direction can be set by selecting the <strong>Run type mapping in reverse order</strong> check box in the third step of the Model Transformation Wizard. This is useful, when there are two related opposite transformations for some domain; for example, the same type map is used for both UML to XML schema and XML schema to UML transformations.</p><p>If you want to limit the directions, in which type map can be used, you can set the<strong> defaultDirection</strong> tag for your type map package. Possible values are <strong>forward</strong>, <strong>reverse</strong>, and <strong>both</strong> (default).</p><p>The direction can also be limited on a per-rule basis. This is controlled by setting the <strong>direction</strong> tag on the type map dependency. Then the mapping rule is excluded from the rule set when the type map is run in a different direction than specified for this rule.</p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142471 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00230: Controlling type inheritance, any, and empty types

- page_id: `189142471`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142471/Controlling+type+inheritance+any+and+empty+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:39:16.326+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Tools > Model Transformation Wizard > Controlling Type Mapping Rule Behavior
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can also control mapping behavior for the type inheritance. By default, derived subtypes are also mapped by the rule governing the parent type (unless, of course, they have their own rules for mapping). If the **blockInheritedSourceTypes** tagged value is set, derived types are not affected by this rule. Let’s review the following example:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Here T1, T2, and T5 are types in the source domain, while T2 and T4 are types in the destination domain. Given these two mappings (T1 > T2 and T3 > T4), the following statement is true: T1, and all types derived from it (such as T5), are mapped to the T2 type, except T3 and any of the types derived from it. These types are 
mapped to T4.

Now consider an example where blockInheritedSourceTypes is set:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In this case, T3, along with the types derived from it, are still mapped to T4. T1 is still mapped to T2. However, unlike the previous example, T5 and all the types inherited from T1 are NOT mapped to T2.

You can also control the mapping behavior of the type inheritance in the destination model. This is only effective on the transformation updates, the second (and successive) reapplications of the transformation. By default, derived subtypes in the destination model are not overwritten, since they are considered suitable substitutes of their parent. Let’s review the following example:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Here T1 is a type in the source domain, while T2 and T4 are types in the destination domain. Given this mapping (T1 > T2), on the first application of the transformation, type T1 residing in the source model will be mapped to type T2 in the destination model.

Now let us look at a case, where the user refines the destination model by changing the type on the destination model attribute from T2 to T4. This situation is quite common, for example, the user refines an attribute type from string to basic URI in the XML schema, or from Integer to *nonNegativeInteger*, and so forth. The essence is that the mapping for inherited types of T2 is performed as if there was a mapping T1 > T2 (default), T1 > T4, T1 > <any_other_type_inherited_from_T2>.

Now consider what happens, when we apply the **blockInheritedDestinationTypes** tagged value:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

In this case, type T4 has no special treatment. If the user applies the transformation, T1 is mapped to T2. Afterwards the user refines the destination model, changing the attribute type from T2 to T4. If the user now updates the transformation, the attribute type is overwritten: T4 is reset back to T2.

When the user loads the type map in the reverse direction, the roles of the **blockInheritedSourceTypes** and **blockInheritedDestinationTypes** are transposed (unless of course the **direction** tag mandates that this mapping is not used in the reverse direction).

The special type **EmptySourceType** (residing in the *Model_Transformation_Profile.xml.zip*) is used in type maps to indicate that the attributes with no type should be mapped with this dependency.

The special type **EmptyDestinationType** (residing in the *Model_Transformation_Profile.xml.zip*) is used to indicate that the attributes in the destination classes should have no type after remapping (type removal).

The special type **AnySourceType** is a template that matches any type in the source model (see mapping rules for type inheritance). By using this type, together with the inheritance mapping rules, the user can specify that any other types not defined by the mapping should be interpreted by this mapping.

The special type **AnyDestinationType** is a template that matches any type in the destination model (see mapping rules for type inheritance).

Here is an example of template type usage:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

According to this rule, any types in the source model for which there are no other mapping rules should be stripped in the destination model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can also control mapping behavior for the type inheritance. By default, derived subtypes are also mapped by the rule governing the parent type (unless, of course, they have their own rules for mapping). If the <strong style="line-height: 1.42857;">blockInheritedSourceTypes</strong> tagged value is set, derived types are not affected by this rule. Let’s review the following example:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Transf_BlockInherited.png" /></ac:image></p><p>Here T1, T2, and T5 are types in the source domain, while T2 and T4 are types in the destination domain. Given these two mappings (T1 &gt; T2 and T3 &gt; T4), the following statement is true: T1, and all types derived from it (such as T5), are mapped to the T2 type, except T3 and any of the types derived from it. These types are <br />mapped to T4.</p><p>Now consider an example where blockInheritedSourceTypes is set:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Transf_BlockInherited1.png" /></ac:image></p><p>In this case, T3, along with the types derived from it, are still mapped to T4. T1 is still mapped to T2. However, unlike the previous example, T5 and all the types inherited from T1 are NOT mapped to T2.</p><p>You can also control the mapping behavior of the type inheritance in the destination model. This is only effective on the transformation updates, the second (and successive) reapplications of the transformation. By default, derived subtypes in the destination model are not overwritten, since they are considered suitable substitutes of their parent. Let’s review the following example:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Transf_BlockInheritedDest.png" /></ac:image></p><p>Here T1 is a type in the source domain, while T2 and T4 are types in the destination domain. Given this mapping (T1 &gt; T2), on the first application of the transformation, type T1 residing in the source model will be mapped to type T2 in the destination model.</p><p>Now let us look at a case, where the user refines the destination model by changing the type on the destination model attribute from T2 to T4. This situation is quite common, for example, the user refines an attribute type from string to basic URI in the XML schema, or from Integer to <em>nonNegativeInteger</em>, and so forth. The essence is that the mapping for inherited types of T2 is performed as if there was a mapping T1 &gt; T2 (default), T1 &gt; T4, T1 &gt; &lt;any_other_type_inherited_from_T2&gt;.</p><p>Now consider what happens, when we apply the <strong>blockInheritedDestinationTypes</strong> tagged value:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Transf_BlockInheritedDest1.png" /></ac:image></p><p>In this case, type T4 has no special treatment. If the user applies the transformation, T1 is mapped to T2. Afterwards the user refines the destination model, changing the attribute type from T2 to T4. If the user now updates the transformation, the attribute type is overwritten: T4 is reset back to T2.</p><p>When the user loads the type map in the reverse direction, the roles of the <strong>blockInheritedSourceTypes</strong> and <strong>blockInheritedDestinationTypes</strong> are transposed (unless of course the <strong>direction</strong> tag mandates that this mapping is not used in the reverse direction).</p><p>The special type <strong>EmptySourceType</strong> (residing in the <em>Model_Transformation_Profile.xml.zip</em>) is used in type maps to indicate that the attributes with no type should be mapped with this dependency.</p><p>The special type <strong>EmptyDestinationType</strong> (residing in the <em>Model_Transformation_Profile.xml.zip</em>) is used to indicate that the attributes in the destination classes should have no type after remapping (type removal).</p><p>The special type <strong>AnySourceType</strong> is a template that matches any type in the source model (see mapping rules for type inheritance). By using this type, together with the inheritance mapping rules, the user can specify that any other types not defined by the mapping should be interpreted by this mapping.</p><p>The special type <strong>AnyDestinationType</strong> is a template that matches any type in the destination model (see mapping rules for type inheritance).</p><p>Here is an example of template type usage:</p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Transf_AnySourceType.png" /></ac:image></p><p>According to this rule, any types in the source model for which there are no other mapping rules should be stripped in the destination model.</p><p> </p>
````

## PAGE 00233: Conversion with Array Type Modifiers

- page_id: `189145770`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145770/Conversion+with+Array+Type+Modifiers
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:42:00.678+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > C++ Code Engineering > Conversion from an Earlier Project Version
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1398284398

1398284400

1398284399

With version 12, type modifiers use the character *$* to specify the type modifiers construct. This allows mapping of complex type modifiers. For example,*const int* const*is mapped to*const $* const*.

##### Type Modifier conversion in Attribute

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of Type Modifier conversion in Attribute

To convert a Type modifier in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Attribute' space='']:

1. Open the Attribute specification window.
2. Remove *[15]* from **Type Modifier** field.
3. Apply the «C++Attribute» stereotype.
4. Set **Multiplicity** field to 0..*. [IMAGE alt='' src='']
5. Go to Tags and set Array tag value to *[15]*.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Type Modifier conversion in Parameter

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of Type Modifier conversion in Parameter

To convert a Type modifier in Parameter:

1. Open the Parameter specification window.
2. Remove [15] from Type Modifier field.
3. Apply the «C++Parameter» stereotype.
4. Set Multiplicity field to 0..*. [ATTACHMENT filename='image2018-1-16_17-19-2.png']
5. Go to Tags and set Array tag value to [15] .

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

1398284397

**Related Pages**:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Conversion from an Earlier Project Version' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Code Engineering' space='']

Pages</strong>:</p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="885220a6-f476-4483-be78-2c880b7e0e9c"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Conversion from an Earlier Project Version" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="5bf5e5ea-318b-44dc-ace3-c420c52331b6"><ac:parameter ac:name="page"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Code Engineering" /></ac:link></ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142013 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00234: Converting Elements

- page_id: `189142013`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142013/Converting+Elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:47.041+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Refactoring
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

###### Original element

The original element is the element that will be converted. In other words, it is the conversion source.

###### Conversion target

The conversion target is the element type, to which the original element is going to be converted.

###### Converted element

The converted element is the element that type has been changed during the conversion.

###### Related elements

The related elements are elements having relations to the original element. Related elements can be the following:

- If the original element is a relationship, elements connected by this relationship are considered as related element to relationship.
- If the original element is not a relationship, relationships going from or to the original element are the related elements (such as an association, dependency, generalization, and others).
- Other related elements associated to the original element. They are properties of the original element, such as inner elements, types, and others.

###### Compatible properties

The compatible properties are original element specification that are compatible with the conversion target. The term “properties” includes related elements, inner elements, and all other elements listed in the element Specification window.

Compatible means that the converted element can have or own the particular property or properties of the original element.

###### Incompatible properties

Incompatible properties are original element specification that are incompatible with the conversion target. Incompatible means that the converted element can NOT have or can NOT own the particular property(-ies) of the original element.

Element conversion allows converting one element type to another. To be more specific, the element conversion functionality allows the UML model element conversion from one metaclass to another. For example, a class element can be converted to a use case, component, interface, or other. All compatible properties and all compatible related elements are moved from the source element to the converted element during the conversion.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

- Relationships conversion is available.
- Multiple elements can be selected on the diagram or in the Model Browser to convert them to other types.

INLINE

To convert elements

1. From the selected element's shortcut menu, select Refactor > Convert To . The list with available conversion target types opens.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Select the element type from the commonly used element type list. The element is converted.
  - Select More Elements to see the whole list of available conversion targets. In the opened More Elements dialog, select the element type you need and click OK . The element is converted and selected element type is included as commonly used element type.

As of the modeling tool version 17.0.2, the following commands have been moved under the **Refactor** command in the shortcut menu of the particular element:

- Convert To > Output Pin for the selected Object Node or Input Pin.
- Convert To > Input Pin for the selected Object Node or Output Pin.
- Convert To > Object Node for the selected Input Pin or Output Pin.
- Convert To > Attribute(s) for the selected Association that has defined roles, that is, roles have names.
- Convert To > Association(s) for the selected Attribute. NOTE : The Association(s) command is available only if the attribute(s) type is specified.

- If the Refactor command is unavailable, make sure you have permissions to edit the selected element or related element(s).
- Use Undo to restore the original element and its data.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

In other words, it is the conversion source.</p><h4>Conversion target</h4><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The conversion target is the element type, to which the original element is going to be converted.</p><h4>Converted element</h4><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The converted element is the element that type has been changed during the conversion.</p><h4>Related elements</h4><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The related elements are elements having relations to the original element. Related elements can be the following:</p><ul><li>If the original element is a relationship, elements connected by this relationship are considered as related element to relationship.</li><li>If the original element is not a relationship, relationships going from or to the original element are the related elements (such as an association, dependency, generalization, and others).</li><li>Other related elements associated to the original element. They are properties of the original element, such as inner elements, types, and others. </li></ul><h4>Compatible properties</h4><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The compatible properties are original element specification that are compatible with the conversion target. The term “properties” includes related elements, inner elements, and all other elements listed in the element Specification window.</p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Compatible means that the converted element can have or own the particular property or properties of the original element.</p><h4>Incompatible properties</h4><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">Incompatible properties are original element specification that are incompatible with the conversion target. Incompatible means that the converted element can NOT have or can NOT own the particular property(-ies) of the original element.</p><p><br /></p><p>Element conversion allows converting one element type to another. To be more specific, the element conversion functionality allows the UML model element conversion from one metaclass to another. For example, a class element can be converted to a use case, component, interface, or other. All compatible properties and all compatible related elements are moved from the source element to the converted element during the conversion.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of Package conversion to Component" ac:alt="Example of Package conversion to Component"><ri:attachment ri:filename="converting_elements.png" /></ac:image></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4a3e4086-f155-40bc-844d-971aa8edbea7"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>Relationships conversion is available.</li><li>Multiple elements can be selected on the diagram or in the Model Browser to convert them to other types.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d78db661-8bfb-45d7-a4a7-f7cd89622b9b"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To convert elements</p><hr /><ol><li>From the selected element's shortcut menu, select <strong>Refactor</strong> &gt; <strong>Convert To</strong>. The list with available conversion target types opens.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>Select the element type from the commonly used element type list. The element is converted.</li><li>Select <strong>More Elements</strong> to see the whole list of available conversion targets. In the opened <strong>More Elements</strong> dialog, select the element type you need and click <strong>OK</strong>. The element is converted and selected element type is included as commonly used element type.</li></ul></li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0a456a27-873e-4747-a07d-dd88e376e87d"><ac:rich-text-body><p>As of the modeling tool version 17.0.2, the following commands have been moved under the <strong>Refactor</strong> command in the shortcut menu of the particular element:</p><ul><li><strong>Convert To</strong> &gt; <strong>Output Pin</strong> for the selected Object Node or Input Pin. </li><li><strong>Convert To</strong> &gt; <strong>Input Pin</strong> for the selected Object Node or Output Pin.</li><li><strong>Convert To</strong> &gt; <strong>Object Node</strong> for the selected Input Pin or Output Pin.</li><li><strong>Convert To</strong> &gt; <strong>Attribute(s)</strong> for the selected Association that has defined roles, that is, roles have names.</li><li><strong>Convert To</strong> &gt; <strong>Association(s)</strong> for the selected Attribute. <strong>NOTE</strong>: The Association(s) command is available only if the attribute(s) type is specified.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="104de9ad-feea-4320-8cd5-d8310cb200a9"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>If the <strong>Refactor</strong> command is unavailable, make sure you have permissions to edit the selected element or related element(s).</li><li>Use <strong>Undo</strong> to restore the original element and its data.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4810fea3-739c-4de7-90a8-6fa7ea57512d"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="089ecc4f-400a-472c-916b-c7607d8b8764" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142122 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00235: Copy and paste symbol style

- page_id: `189142122`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142122/Copy+and+paste+symbol+style
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:52.234+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Formatting symbols
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A symbol style defined in symbol properties can be copied and pasted on another symbol. Copy a symbol and then select one or more other symbols of the same type where you need to paste copied properties.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To copy and paste symbol style

1. On the diagram pane, select symbol(s) which style properties you want to copy.
2. Copy style properties by doing one of the following:
  - From the Edit menu, select Copy .
  - Press Ctrl+C.
  - Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='copy.png'] Copy button on the diagram toolbar.
3. On the diagram pane, select the symbol(s) on which you want to paste style properties.
4. Paste style properties on the selected symbols by doing one of the following:
  - From the Edit menu, select Paste Style .
  - Press Ctrl+Shift+V.
  - Click the [ATTACHMENT filename='paste_style.png'] Paste Style button on the diagram toolbar.

You can copy and paste symbol properties of the more than one symbol at the time. Symbol properties will be pasted according to the element type.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

true[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Formatting symbols' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142280 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00236: Copying and opening element URLs

- page_id: `189142280`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142280/Copying+and+opening+element+URLs
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:39:08.890+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1213345435

1213345437

1213345436

You can copy a project element URL to a clipboard and share it with others as a quick reference to model elements.

Copying and opening element URLs do not work with the no install version (.zip) of a modeling tool.

To copy a project element URL, do one of the following

- Select Copy URL from the element shortcut menu in the Containment tree to copy the URL to a model element.
- Select the element symbol in a diagram and click Edit > Copy URL on the main menu to copy the URL to element symbol.

You can open any element through its URL by clicking the **Open Element from URL** command. The element will then be highlighted in the Containment tree or in the diagram. A custom URL "mdel://" is registered into windows registry. Activating the URL in other applications will allow you to start the modeling tool, open the project (if possible), and select one or more elements. You can paste URLs from the clipboard to any modeling tool diagram. Hyperlinks also can hold URLs of any model elements.

You cannot open elements using their URLs on an internet browser to start a modeling tool.

1213345434

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with model elements' space='']

The element will then be highlighted in the Containment tree or in the diagram. A custom URL &quot;mdel://&quot; is registered into windows registry. Activating the URL in other applications will allow you to start the modeling tool, open the project (if possible), and select one or more elements. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140694 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00237: Copying and pasting data

- page_id: `189140694`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140694/Copying+and+pasting+data
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:08.651+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Basic tasks in tables
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can transfer information by copying and pasting data between tables and other resources, such as Excel spreadsheets, documents, web pages, email messages, and presentations. You are able to copy and paste data:

33

##### From other resources to modeling tool tables

You can copy information from other resources (e.g. Excel spreadsheets, documents, web pages, email messages, presentations) and paste it into a modeling tool table. After pasting the data to the modeling tool table, the new elements are created in the model. If elements already exist in the model, the information updates. The ID prefixes are set automatically after pasting data into modeling tool tables.

The following rules should be satisfied for a successful copy-and-paste operation to modeling tool tables:

- The cells must be editable.
- The number of columns in the other resource should be the same as the number of columns in the modeling tool table.
- The data types of columns in the modeling tool table must be compatible with the copied information from the other resource.

How to copy data from another resource and paste it into a modeling tool:

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Instance table' space='']]]>
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic table' space='']]]>
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Glossary table' space='']]]>

To copy data from another resource and paste it to the modeling tool table

1. Copy the table from another resource. - The number of columns in that table should be the same as the number of columns in the table.- The data types of columns in the table must be compatible with the copied information.
2. Open the table where you want to paste the copied data.
3. In the Criteria area, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to specify the Element Type (in Generic table), Classifier (in Instance table), or Metric Suite (in Metric table).
4. In the table toolbar, click the **Show Columns/Columns** button and choose the columns that you want to fill with the copied information. The cells must be editable.
5. Do one of the following: 
 - Press Ctrl+V (Cmd+V on OS X). 
 - In the main toolbar, select **Edit** > **Paste.**The new elements are created in the model. If the element already exists in the model, the information updates. If elements have an ID prefix in another resource, they are set automatically after the copy-and-paste command only for pasted elements in the table. Manage ID prefixes in the[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Element Numbering dialog' space='']**Element Numbering** dialog.

##### From modeling tool tables to other resources

You can transfer information by copying and pasting data from modeling tool tables to other resources (e.g. Excel spreadsheets, documents, web pages, email messages, presentations).

To copy data from modeling tool tables and paste it to other resources

1. Select the required cells in the modeling tool table.
2. Do one of the following: - Press Ctrl+C (Cmd+C on OS X). - In the main toolbar, select Edit > Copy . The data is stored in the clipboard.
3. Open the other resource where you want to paste the data and select the first cell in the area you want to paste what you copied (e.g. Excel spreadsheet).
4. Press Ctrl+V. The contents of the cells are transferred to another resource.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Between cells in the modeling tool table

You can fill the table cells by copying the content from the modeling tool table cell(s) and pasting it to another cell(s) in the same or another modeling tool table(s). After pasting the data to a modeling tool table the new elements are created in the model. If elements already exist in the model, the information updates. The ID prefixes are set automatically after pasting data into modeling tool tables.

The following rules should be satisfied for a successful copy-and-paste operation to modeling tool tables:

- The cells must be editable.
- The data types of columns in the modeling tool table must be compatible with copied information.
- The cells contain the following data types: String, Boolean, Number, or Enumeration.

Copy and paste cell content into a modeling tool table

1. Select the cells that contain the data you want to copy.
2. Do one of the following: - Press Ctrl+C (Cmd+C on OS X). - In the main toolbar, select Edit > Copy . The data is stored in the clipboard.
3. Select single or multiple cells where you want to paste what you copied.
4. Do one of the following: - Press Ctrl+V (Cmd+V on OS X). - In the main toolbar, select Edit > Paste. The content of the cell(s) is duplicated in another cell(s).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

click the <strong>Show Columns/Columns</strong> button and choose the columns that you want to fill with the copied information.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="4c07b112-a00b-4972-be88-7dd778d0bef0"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The cells must be editable.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Do one of the following:<br /> - Press Ctrl+V (Cmd+V on OS X).<br /> - In the main toolbar, select <strong>Edit</strong> &gt; <strong>Paste.<br /></strong>The new elements are created in the model. If the element already exists in the model, the information updates.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="91ed84e9-89ff-46a9-a83e-5992bd300d7e"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If elements have an ID prefix in another resource, they are set automatically after the copy-and-paste command only for pasted elements in the table. Manage ID prefixes in the<ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Element Numbering dialog" /><ac:link-body> <strong>Element Numbering</strong> dialog</ac:link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><h3>From modeling tool tables to other resources</h3><p>You can transfer information by copying and pasting data from modeling tool tables to other resources (e.g. Excel spreadsheets, documents, web pages, email messages, presentations).</p><p><br /></p><p>To copy data from modeling tool tables and paste it to other resources</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the required cells in the modeling tool table.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br /> - Press Ctrl+C (Cmd+C on OS X).<br /> - In the main toolbar, select <strong>Edit</strong> &gt; <strong>Copy</strong>.<br />The data is stored in the clipboard.</li><li>Open the other resource where you want to paste the data and select the first cell in the area you want to paste what you copied (e.g. Excel spreadsheet).</li><li>Press Ctrl+V.<br />The contents of the cells are transferred to another resource.</li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="copy_paste_operation_from MD_table_to_excel.png" /></ac:image></p><h3>Between cells in the modeling tool table</h3><p>You can fill the table cells by copying the content from the modeling tool table cell(s) and pasting it to another cell(s) in the same or another modeling tool table(s). After pasting the data to a modeling tool table the new elements are created in the model. If elements already exist in the model, the information updates. The ID prefixes are set automatically after pasting data into modeling tool tables.</p><p>The following rules should be satisfied for a successful copy-and-paste operation to modeling tool tables:</p><ul><li>The cells must be editable.</li><li><p>The data types of columns in the modeling tool table must be compatible with copied information.</p></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">The cells contain the following data types: String, Boolean, Number, or Enumeration.</p></li></ul><p>Copy and paste cell content into a modeling tool table</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the cells that contain the data you want to copy.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br /> - Press Ctrl+C (Cmd+C on OS X).<br /> - In the main toolbar, select <strong>Edit</strong> &gt; <strong>Copy</strong>.<br />The data is stored in the clipboard.</li><li>Select single or multiple cells where you want to paste what you copied.</li><li>Do one of the following:<br /> - Press Ctrl+V (Cmd+V on OS X).<br /> - In the main toolbar, select <strong>Edit</strong> &gt; <strong>Paste.</strong><br />The content of the cell(s) is duplicated in another cell(s).</li></ol><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="copy_paste_operation_between_cells_in_MD_table.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p><p style="text-align: right;"><ac:link ac:anchor="top"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Back to Top]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140909 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00238: Copying and pasting objects

- page_id: `189140909`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140909/Copying+and+pasting+objects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:18.863+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1422299706

1422299708

1422299707

Modeling tools support all common copying/cutting and pasting functions, as well as advanced copying and pasting functions. You can copy and paste objects from one project to another. You can find these options in the **Edit** menu.

Advanced commands for copying:

| Command | Shortcut | Description |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Windows | Mac OS |  |
| Copy URL |  |  | Copies the URL of the selected project element or element symbol to the clipboard (the project element and the element symbol have different URLs). To copy the URL of an element, select Copy URL from the element shortcut menu in the Containment tree. To copy the URL of an element symbol, select the the symbol and click Edit > Copy URL.You can use URLs to open any elements by clicking the Open Element from URL command. The element is then highlighted in the Containment tree or in a diagram. Activating the URL in another application allows you to start the modeling tool, open a project (if possible), and select any elements. You can paste URLs from the clipboard to any diagram. Hyperlinks can also hold URLs of any model elements. |
| Copy as BMP Image | Ctrl+Shift+B | ⌘⇧B | Copies the selected shapes to the clipboard. If no shapes are selected, the active diagram is copied. The .bmp image format provides the best image quality. The image files of this format are typically not compressed, resulting in a large file. |
| Copy as EMF Image | Ctrl+Shift+E | ⌘⇧E | Copies the selected shapes to the clipboard. If no shapes are selected, the active diagram is copied. The .emf format image files are smaller, so using this format reduces network traffic. Additionally, the .emf format supports language specific symbols.EMF does not support gradient fill. Because icons of the modeling tool are stored in the SVG format, it may be exported incorrectly.To render SVG icons as raster images in EMF, on the main menu, click Options > Environment > General > Image Export and set the Render SVG Icons as Raster Images in EMF option to true. |
| Copy as JPG Image | Ctrl+Shift+J | ⌘⇧J | Copies the selected shapes to the clipboard. If no shapes are selected, the active diagram is copied. The .jpg format produces relatively small file sizes, but when compressing, the .jpg image file loses data, which results in lower quality. Thus, this format is suitable for final distribution of images, but is not recommended if images are to be edited. |
| Copy as PNG Image | Ctrl+Shift+P | ⌘⇧P | Copies the selected shapes to the clipboard. If no shapes are selected, the active diagram is copied. The .png format is best suited for storing images during the editing process because of its lossless compression. Compared to the .jpg format, the .png excels when the image has large areas of uniform color. It also works well in online viewing applications, such as web browsers. |

Advanced commands for pasting:

| Command | Shortcut | Description |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | Windows | Mac OS |  |
| Paste with New Element | Ctrl+E | ⌘E | Pastes one or more copied model elements by creating new model elements. Use this command when copying or cutting owned symbols from one shape to another in a diagram. |
| Paste Style | Ctrl+Shift+V | ⌘⇧V | Pastes symbol properties to the selected symbol. You can copy and paste symbol properties of more than one symbol at the same time. Symbol properties are pasted according to the element type. For example, on a class symbol, the class symbol properties are pasted, and on a package symbol, the package symbol properties are pasted, etc. |
| Paste as Diagram Overview |  |  | Creates a new diagram overview representing selected elements. A diagram overview shape provides the ability to overview other diagrams on a diagram pane. Moreover, using diagram overview shape you can overview dependency matrices, tables, and relation maps. |

In addition to the described commands, there you have the ability to copy the whole diagram or the selected shapes and paste them to Microsoft Office or other applications.

Not all symbols can be copied/cut and pasted.

1422299705

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Saving as image' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141101 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00239: Copying text or images to diagrams

- page_id: `189141101`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141101/Copying+text+or+images+to+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:51.085+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

570108383

570108385

570108384

You can copy the text or images to a diagram. The copied text or image is pasted into a text box or an image shape. The modeling tool supports the HTML and plain text, and images of gif, jpg, svg, and png image file types. This copy and paste functionality allows you to drag and drop data from other applications, for example, web browsers.

To copy the text or image to a diagram

1. Select the text or image.
2. Press Ctrl+C.
3. Open the diagram.
4. Press Ctrl+V. The Paste Special dialog opens.
5. Select Image , Plain Text , or HTML Text according to what you want to paste on the diagram.

570108382

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']

This copy and paste functionality allows you to drag and drop data from other applications, for example, web browsers.</p><p><br /></p><p>To copy the text or image to a diagram</p><hr /><ol><li>Select the text or image.</li><li>Press Ctrl+C.</li><li>Open the diagram.</li><li>Press Ctrl+V. The <strong>Paste Special</strong> dialog opens.</li><li>Select <strong>Image</strong>, <strong>Plain Text</strong>, or <strong>HTML Text</strong> according to what you want to paste on the diagram.</li></ol></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="54003f95-1740-4eec-bac5-3ee0534d93e4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">570108382</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Diagramming" /></ac:link><strong><br class="_mce_tagged_br" /></strong></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

## PAGE 00240: CORBA IDL diagram

- page_id: `189140495`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140495/CORBA+IDL+diagram
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:00.223+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Extension diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

#### CONTENT-LAYER: Functionalities

135158942084

INLINE

#### CONTENT-COLUMN: Functionalities

345982345

INLINE

#### CONTENT-BLOCK: Functionalities

23458729345

INLINE

#### NOTE: Functionalities

Functionalities

This feature is available in Architect and Enterprise editions.

The CORBA IDL diagram facilitates the creation of CORBA IDL elements. The following patterns are also available for CORBA IDL: Interface, Value Type, Type Definition, Sequence, Array, Fixed, Union, Enumeration, Struct, and Exception.

You can select either the UML Interface or the UML Class as a base element for the CORBA Interface. For more information about the CORBA IDL Interface implementation, see CORBA Interface Implementation.

Reference: UMLTMProfile for CORBATMSpecification, Version 1.0, April 2002.[http://www.omg.org/technology/documents/formal/profile_corba.htm](http://www.omg.org/technology/documents/formal/profile_corba.htm)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of the CORBA IDL diagram *Tickets Purchasing*

43578293475

INLINE

**Related Pages:**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Extension diagrams' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagramming' space='']
