<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145856 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00241: CORBA IDL Mapping To UML

- page_id: `189145856`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145856/CORBA+IDL+Mapping+To+UML
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:42:03.770+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

**Reference:**

[http://www.omg.org/technology/documents/profile_catalog.htm#UML_for_CORBA](http://www.omg.org/technology/documents/profile_catalog.htm#UML_for_CORBA).

CORBA IDL mapping to UML is based on the UML Profile for CORBA Specification, Version 1.0, April 2002.

#### INFO: Availability

Availability

This functionality is available in the Enterprise edition only.

The differences between the UML Profile for CORBA specification and mapping in the modeling tool are listed below.

- Constraints defined in the UML Profile for CORBA specification are not checked in the modeling tool.
- The stereotype CORBAAnonymousFixed is introduced. It is used to represent anonymous fixed types of a stereotype. Fixed types without names are mapped to inner classes with the stereotype CORBAAnonymousFixed. These classes are bound to CORBA::fixed classes.IDL Code:

#### TIP: Example

Example

Code:

```xml
high_scale;

fixed <8, 2> low_scale;

};]]>
```

This code is mapped to the following diagram: [IMAGE alt='' src='']

Modeling tool presents a CORBA IDL diagram that simplifies the creation of standard CORBA IDL elements. The following elements are available in the CORBA IDL diagram:

| Button | Shortcut Key | Model Element |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | M | CORBA IDL Module |
|  | I | CORBA IDL Interface |
|  | V | CORBA IDL Value |
|  | Shift + P | Class by Pattern |
|  | G | Generalization |
|  |  | Truncatable Generalization |
|  |  | Value Support Generalization |
|  |  | CORBA IDL Association |
|  | I | Interface |

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145867 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00242: CORBA interface implementation

- page_id: `189145867`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145867/CORBA+interface+implementation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:42:03.921+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Code Engineering > CORBA IDL Mapping To UML
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can select the UML Interface or the UML Class as a base element for the CORBA Interface. The default element as a base element for CORBA Interfaces is:

- **UML Class**: a class as a base element for CORBA Interfaces appears in projects created in modeling tool version 17.0.1. The CORBA Interfaces are modeled as classes in these projects.
- **UML Interface**: an interface as a base element for CORBA Interfaces appears in projects created in modeling tool version 17.0.1 and later. The CORBA Interfaces are modeled as interfaces in these projects.

#### WARNING: Important!

Important!

The generalization relationship can be used only between CORBA Interfaces based on the same element. Thus, you can use the generalization relationship between two CORBA Intarfaces based on a class or between two CORBA Interfaces based on an interface, but not between a CORBA Interface based on a class and a CORBA Interface based on an interface.

To set a base element for CORBA Interfaces

1.On the**Options**menu, click**Project**. The**Project Options**dialog opens.

2.In the Tab tree, select**General**.

3.In the**General**list, click the**CORBA IDL**and then **CORBA Interfaces implemented as**value cell.The list of available values opens.

4.Do one of the following:

- Select UML Class to set a class as the base element for the CORBA Interface.
- Select UML Interface to set an interface as the base element for the CORBA Interface.

5.Click**OK**when you are done.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To change a base element of CORBA Interfaces

1.Select the CORBA Interface element in the Model Browser or its symbol on the diagram pane.

2.From the shortcut menu of the selected CORBA Interface, point to**Refactor**>**Convert To**, and then do one of the following:

- Click Class if an interface is the current base element for CORBA Interface.
- Click Interface if a class is the current base element for CORBA Interface.

#### WARNING: Important!

Important!

Convert the selected CORBA Interface only to the class or interface element. Conversion to other elements changes the CORBA Interfaceelement to the selected element but not to the element the CORBA Interface is based on.

For more information about refactoring, see [Refactoring](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2022x/Refactoring).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

The CORBA Interfaces are modeled as classes in these projects.</span></li><li><span><strong>UML Interface</strong>: an interface as a base element for CORBA Interfaces appears in projects created in modeling tool version 17.0.1 and later. The CORBA Interfaces are modeled as interfaces in these projects.</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="97969abe-4cfa-49ea-8dc5-12e725e1e54f"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Important!</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body>The generalization relationship can be used only between CORBA Interfaces based on the same element. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141929 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00243: Covering use cases of Excel Import Plugin

- page_id: `189141929`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141929/Covering+use+cases+of+Excel+Import+Plugin
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:45:32.418+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Importing data from Excel or CSV files
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page is dedicated for those who used the Excel Import Plugin for data import to the model. As of 2021x version this plugin is deprecated and import functionality is covered by [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space=''] (import to table) and [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing data from Excel or CSV files' space=''] (import to model) features. To explain the mapping of deprecated Excel Import Plugin, read the following sections:

5

##### Removing auto usages

Before importing data from Excel or CSV files by using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing data from Excel or CSV files' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space=''] features, you must remove auto usages of Excel Import Plugin, since 20.0 version its no longer available to use. [Learn more about automated project usages >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Automated+project+usages)

To remove the Excel Import Plugin usage from your project

1. Open the project.
2. In the Containment tree, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] and select Show Auxiliary Resources .
3. Right-click an Excel Import Profile package in the Containment tree and, from its shortcut menu, select Project Usages > Remove. After removal, all the elements of the used project disappear from the Containment tree, and all the references to these elements are cleared from other model places.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### Preparing the Excel spreadsheet

Before importing data from Excel file by using the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Importing data from Excel or CSV files' space=''] or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sync with Excel or CSV files' space=''] features, we recommend to make sure the data in your Excel file is in line. As shown in the following figure, the merged cells are eliminated by showing nested structure in line. [Learn more about preparing Excel file to import >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Importing+data+from+Excel+or+CSV+files#ImportingdatafromExcelorCSVfiles-PreparingExcelorCSVfiletoimport)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The comparison of Excel tables available to import by using the Excel Import Plugin and Excel/CSV Sync or Excel/CSV Import features.

##### User interface comparison

To import data from Excel or CSV file by using the Excel Import Plugin, three dialogs were used to import table heading, create mapping and import data. The same cases are covered with one dialog depending where you want to import data:

- To the table use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Sync Options dialog' space='']**Excel/CSV Sync Options** dialog .
- To the model use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Excel and CSV Import dialog' space='']**Excel/CSV Import** dialog .

You cannot create a diagram when importing data by using the Excel/CSV Sync or the Excel/CSV Import features.

The figure below illustrates the differences between dialogs of Excel Import Plugin and **Excel/CSV Sync Options** or **Excel/CSV Import**.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The UI differences between Excel Import Plugin and Excel/CSV Sync and Excel/CSV Import.

##### Mapping comparison

The Excel Import Plugin allows you to create mapping in the **Create Mapping** dialog that generates the class diagram. In the **Excel/CSV Sync Options** dialog you create mapping directly in the dialog. The comparison of the Excel Import Plugin and Excel/CSV Sync mappings are provided in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The mapping differences between Excel Import Plugin and Excel/CSV Sync.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141843 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00244: Creating a basic flow

- page_id: `189141843`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141843/Creating+a+basic+flow
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:36.367+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Use Case > Use Case Scenario sketch
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

795872164

795872166

795872165

INLINE

To create a basic flow

1. Open the Use Case Specification window and click the Use Case Scenario Sketch property group.
2. In the Basic Flow area, click the Add new step button [ATTACHMENT filename='add_new_value_button.png'] or press Alt+ENTER and type a name of the basic flow step. Repeat this action for each basic flow step you need to create.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

Steps of the basic flow are numbered automatically. Use the Up [IMAGE alt='' src='']and Down [IMAGE alt='' src=''] buttons to change the order of steps. In the activity diagram, steps are represented according to their order in the Use Case scenario.

795872163

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Transition' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='State Machine diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Activity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Sequence diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Use Case diagram' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="95943085-9713-4722-b81f-cabff034c66b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872164</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1b2d0119-796a-44ac-9226-0cc70f7bd30b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872166</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="d51170af-9238-4b08-873e-3acf6080e2e9"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872165</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ae3e3ae2-3574-4f5f-bc69-479444538c60"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>To create a basic flow</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the Use Case Specification window and click the <strong>Use Case Scenario Sketch </strong>property group.</li><li>In the <strong>Basic Flow </strong>area, click the Add new step button <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_new_value_button.png" /></ac:image> or press Alt+ENTER and type a name of the basic flow step. In the activity diagram, steps are represented according to their order in the Use Case scenario.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="093459a2-0bf2-4020-9a33-7f585c68705f"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">795872163</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="UML elements" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Model Elements]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Transition" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="State Machine diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Activity diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Sequence diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Use Case diagram" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140923 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00245: Creating a diagram overview shape

- page_id: `189140923`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140923/Creating+a+diagram+overview+shape
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:21.021+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Overviewing other diagrams
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

569216953

569216957

569216955

You can use one of the following ways to create a diagram overview shape:

- Drag the diagram shape from the Model Browser and change it into the diagram overview shape afterward . This way created diagram overview shape shows the full content of the corresponding diagram and is updated automatically according to all changes made in this diagram. [ATTACHMENT filename='dgr_and_dgr_overview_shape_full.png']
- **Use the [IMAGE alt='' src='']Diagram Overview button that is located in the diagram palette**. This way created diagram overview shape shows the full content of the corresponding diagram and is updated automatically according to all changes made in this diagram.
- **Paste a copied part of the diagram’s content using a special command from the main menu**. This way created diagram overview shape shows any copied part of the corresponding diagram’s content and is updated automatically according only to *the changes made in this copied part*. Note that this way cannot be used for dependency matrices, tables, and relation maps, as it is not allowed to copy and paste different parts of them. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To create a diagram overview shape by dragging it to the diagram pane

1. Open a diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.
2. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] select a diagram you want to overview.
3. Drag the diagram shape to the open diagram pane. If you cannot drag the diagram shape to the open diagram pane when working with a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.
4. Make the diagram shape show the full content of the corresponding diagram by doing one of the following:
  - Right-click the diagram shape, and from the shortcut menu, select [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Symbol Properties dialog' space=''] . In the open dialog, set the Show Diagram Overview Content property value to true . [ATTACHMENT filename='show_dgr_overview_content_symbol_properties.png']
  - Click the diagram shape, and on the smart manipulator toolbar, select the [IMAGE alt='' src='']button. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
The diagram shape will be changed to the diagram overview shape that shows the full content of the corresponding diagram. The content of the diagram overview shape will be updated automatically according to*all changes* made in the corresponding diagram. Double-click the diagram overview shape to open the corresponding diagram.

To create a diagram overview shape by using the diagram palette

1. Open a diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.
2. On the diagram palette, click the **Diagram Overview** button that is located within the **Common** buttons group. The **Select Diagram** dialog is open. If the diagram palette is inactive within a Teamwork Cloud project, make sure you have the right to edit the model of this project and then try to lock for edit this diagram.
3. Select a diagram for which you want to create a diagram overview shape and click OK .
4. Click a free space of the diagram pane.

To create a diagram overview shape by pasting any part of the diagram’s content

1. Select the element shapes that you want to appear in the diagram overview shape and then copy them.
2. Open the diagram wherein you want to create the diagram overview shape.
3. From the **Edit** menu, select **Paste as Diagram Overview**. The content will be updated automatically according to the changes made in the copied part of the corresponding diagram only.

569216952

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Modifying the content of a diagram overview shape' space='MD185']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145493 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00246: Creating a DSL Element from the Customized Category in the Shortcut Menu

- page_id: `189145493`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145493/Creating+a+DSL+Element+from+the+Customized+Category+in+the+Shortcut+Menu
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:46:14.646+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The **Create Element** menu in the Model Browser allows you to group elements into categories. You can also use various menus in dialogs and specifications to group elements. Use the **Category** property of a customization element to create categories.

**Case Study**

The following example demonstrates how to create a category for a new requirement element within a Package element. This example refers to the project described in [Case Study: Creating Your First Customization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Creating+your+First+Customization).

1. Assign the custom stereotype (in this case the *«Company»* stereotype) as a **Customization Target** property in the customization element Specification window. Specify the Package metaclass as a **Possible Owners** property value. Please see the [Case Study: Creating Your First Customization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Creating+your+First+Customization) for more details.
2. In the customization element Specification window: [IMAGE alt='' src=''] In this example, we used the stereotype created in the example described in [Case Study: Creating Your First Customization](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Creating+your+First+Customization).
  - specify the name of a new category as the **Category** property value (for example, *Requirements)*.
  - specify the name of an element to create as the **Representation text**property value (for example, *Business Requirements)*.
3. Save and reopen your project.
4. The *Business Requirement* element is added to the package shortcut menu, as shown below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The custom Requirements category in the **Create Element**menu.

To create several items under the same category, you must create a customization element for each item. Specify the same name of the **Category** property.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145500 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00247: Creating a DSL Element from the Shortcut Menu and Other Locations

- page_id: `189145500`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145500/Creating+a+DSL+Element+from+the+Shortcut+Menu+and+Other+Locations
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:46:14.959+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

220200288

220200291

220200290

The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating your First Customization' space=''] section introduced the step-by-step process of creating a custom element. Following are tips to create custom elements.

Use the **Possible Owners** property to define the possible owner for a customized element. You can also quickly create a customized element from the possible owner shortcut menu in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] and in other locations. For example, you can add a package as a possible owner for a customized use case element.

Use the **Possible Owners** property to quickly create a customized element from the following locations:

- The possible owner’s shortcut menu, under the Create Element command, in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space='']
- The Inner Elements property group under the possible owner’s Specification window.
- The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting elements' space='']**Select Element** dialog , when the element creation mode is turned on.

Examples of creating the *Business Requirement*element in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating a DSL Element from the Customized Category in the Shortcut Menu' space=''] are shown below:

- In the package shortcut menu (in the Model Browser) under the Create Element command. [ATTACHMENT filename='package_shortcut_menu.png']
- In the possible owner’s Specification window, the Inner Elements property group. [ATTACHMENT filename='creating_in_specification_window.png']
- In the element selection dialog, when creating a new elem ent. [ATTACHMENT filename='select_owner_dialog_creation_mode.png']

220200287

**Related Pages**

- Creating Customization Data
- Using Customization Data
- Customization Element Properties Description

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145882 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00248: Creating a Macro

- page_id: `189145882`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145882/Creating+a+Macro
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:42:04.672+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > Macro Engine Documentation
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Once you have selected a default macro language (see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting a default macro language' space='']), you can create a new macro by using the **Create Macro** dialog, see figure below. The dialog allows you to specify a macro language, enter source code, and save it.

To create a new macro

1. Click Tools > Macros > Create Macro on the main menu. The Create Macro dialog opens. [ATTACHMENT filename='create-macro.png']
2. Select a macro language in the **Macro Language** box. To ensure the best performance experience, it is strongly suggested that you use the recommended language (Groovy). The icon color next to the language selection reflects performance, (i.e., green for fast, yellow for medium, and red for slow). If the performance is unknown, an info icon appears.
3. Enter the source code in the text box.
4. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to test and make sure that the source code works properly.
5. Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] . The Macro Information dialog opens. Follow the steps described on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Adding a Macro and editing Macro information' space=''] page.
6. After clicking OK in the Macro Information dialog, the new macro will be saved in the location you have specified in the File text box.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Once you have selected a default macro language (see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Selecting a default macro language" /></ac:link>), you can create a new macro by using the <strong>Create Macro</strong> dialog, see figure below. The dialog allows you to specify a macro language, enter source code, and save it.</p><p>To create a new macro</p><hr /><ol><li>Click <strong>Tools</strong> &gt; <strong>Macros</strong> &gt; <strong>Create Macro</strong> on the main menu. The<strong> Create Macro</strong> dialog opens.<br /><ac:image ac:title="The Create Macro Dialog" ac:alt="The Create Macro Dialog"><ri:attachment ri:filename="create-macro.png" /></ac:image><br /><br /></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"> Select a macro language in the <strong>Macro Language</strong> box.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="08c8f1bf-cf76-4a9e-a576-3bba18f3c988"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To ensure the best performance experience, it is strongly suggested that you use the recommended language (Groovy). The icon color next to the language selection reflects performance, (i.e., green for fast, yellow for medium, and red for slow). If the performance is unknown, an info icon appears.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li><li> Enter the source code in the text box.</li><li> Click <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="run-btn.png" /></ac:image></strong> to test and make sure that the source code works properly.</li><li> Click <strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="save-btn.png" /></ac:image></strong>. The <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog opens. Follow the steps described on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Adding a Macro and editing Macro information" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Adding a Macro and Its Information]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> page.</li><li> After clicking <strong>OK</strong> in the <strong>Macro Information</strong> dialog, the new macro will be saved in the location you have specified in the <strong>File</strong> text box.</li></ol>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140815 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00249: Creating a Metric Table

- page_id: `189140815`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140815/Creating+a+Metric+Table
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:12.823+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Metric table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Although you can create other types of tables in various ways, there is only one correct way to create a Metric Table. This method of creating Metric Tables saves much time compared to the more common ways of table (and even diagram) creation, as these methods for a Metric Table creation start with an empty table. This requires further effort on your part to fill it (specify the metric suite, create an empty row, define parameters, and then calculate the metrics).

To create a Metric Table

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space=''], right-click thePackage which contains e.g. requirements, system structure, system behavior, which will be used as a scope for the metrics calculation.
2. From the shortcut menu of the element, select **Tools** > **Metrics** > **New Metric Table**.
3. In the **Create Metric Table** dialog, specify the following: 
 - Type the Metric Table name; 
 - Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to specify the Metric Table owner.By default, the selected Package in the Containment tree is chosen. 
 - From the list of available metric suites, select the check boxes next to the Metric Suites you want to use.There are three groups of predefined Metric Suites: If predefined Metric Suites do not allow to evaluate the current state of your model, you can create a custom Metric Suite according to your needs.[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Metric Suites' space='']>]]> [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Metric Suite descriptionsSelect a Metric Suite and click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] to read its description in the description area of the **Create Metric Table** dialog. Carefully read descriptions of each metric suite. This helps you decide which one best suits your needs.
  - Requirement Metric Suites (Obsolete)
  - Requirement Coverage (Treat Owner As Grouping Element)
  - Requirement Coverage (Treat Owner As Requirement)
4. Click **OK**.

The table of a particular Metric Suite is created for the selected Package. It is already filled with data because metrics calculated at the time you create the table. These metrics are actually stored in the Package owned by the element for which they have been calculated. As example, see the figure below. 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

If one or more metrics are not calculated, make sure the Metric Suite is specified correctly:

- Check the metric definition – the formula could be specified incorrectly.
- Check the parameter definition – the metric definition probably cannot access parameter values.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189139722 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00250: Creating a new project

- page_id: `189139722`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189139722/Creating+a+new+project
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:21.579+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Creating projects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1428335651

1428335654

1428335653

INLINE

All project information is stored in a single file that has the same name as the project itself.

A newly created project consists of the following packages:

- The empty root package Model, storing all model elements.
- File View package, containing components that are created during code engineering and representing source files. Adding a resident element to a particular component causes that element to be generated within the source file.
- UML Standard Profile, containing stereotypes that are necessary in order to work with the modeling tool, primitive data types and constraints (which are UML standard), and UML 2 metamodel elements. The following data types are specified in the modeling tool: boolean, byte, char, date, double, float, int, Integer, real long, short, void, and string.

You can create your own packages for keeping model elements as well. By default, packages cannot be deleted or renamed in a project (except for the *File View* package).

To start a new project, first, you must create a new workspace for it. You can select a project type from the following domains:

- General-Purpose Modeling (UML, Use Case, Guide to UML Diagrams projects)
- System Engineering (SysML project)
- Enterprise Modeling (DoDAF, DoDAF 2.0, MODAF projects)
- Software Engineering (Scrum project, Project from Existing Source Code)
- Business Process Modeling (BPMN 2.0 project)
- Service-Oriented Modeling (Cameo SOA+ project)
- Other (Project from Template, Process Guide project)

Upon creation of a new project, the **General-Purpose Modeling** domain opens by default.

To create a new workspace for a blank project

1. Do one of the following:

- 
  - From the File menu, select New Project .
  - On the main toolbar, click the New Project button.
  - Press Ctrl + Shift + N.

Any of the above actions opens the **New Project** dialog.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

2. Select the **UML Project** in the **General-Purpose Modeling** domain.

3. Specify the file name in the **Name** box.

4. Click the [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button to select the location to store the created project.

5. Click **OK**.

1428335648

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating projects' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Project Properties dialog' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with multiple projects' space='']

## PAGE 00251: Creating a new slide for a presentation

- page_id: `189145064`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145064/Creating+a+new+slide+for+a+presentation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:26.440+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Report Wizard Directives > #forpage directive
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a new slide for a presentation

1. Open OpenOffice.org Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.
2. Create two text boxes.
3. Type in: 
(i) one of the text boxes: text (ii) and type in the other text box: text The result will appear as shown in the figure below.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

The result of the generated report will appear shown in the figure below. 
 
 [IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a new slide for a presentation</p><hr /><ol><li>Open <a href="http://OpenOffice.org">OpenOffice.org</a> Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.</li><li>Create two text boxes.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Type in:<br />(i) one of the text boxes:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="94c10f12-41cb-4d4d-a942-9a270ff64ad8"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[#forpage($dia in $Diagram) 
$dia.name]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target">(ii) and type in the other text box:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a112c55c-f1ea-4b2b-9b37-d8c22d0489ae"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[$dia.image 
#endpage]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The result will appear as shown in the figure below.</p></li></ol><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddavbf2e5609b8cf026cfb71c63ca946bfca.png" /></ac:image></p><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The result of the generated report will appear shown in the figure below.<br /> <br class="atl-forced-newline" /> <ac:image ac:title="A Generated Report" ac:alt="A Generated Report"><ri:attachment ri:filename="worddav8dfe469fffe291fde883d6b38b0573a7.png" /></ac:image></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141062 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00252: Creating a relationship

- page_id: `189141062`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141062/Creating+a+relationship
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:45.008+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Working with paths and relationships
- labels: ['create-relationship', 'create-path', 'relationship-direction', 'relate-source-and-target', 'path-direction', 'path-target']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

You can create relationships between elements in one of the following ways:

- 
- 
- 

diagram_paneTo draw a path on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space='']

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] , click the needed relationship.
2. Select the source shape of the path in the diagram pane.
3. Drop the path to the target shape. When drawing a new path, the optimal route is chosen automatically (the path avoids other shapes).When drawing a path between two elements, those shapes are bordered with a red or blue rectangle. The red highlight indicates that the path can not be drawn between these shapes. The blue highlight allows a path to be drawn. You can disable this highlighting in the **Environment Options** dialog under the Diagram node by setting the **Show Manipulation Highlighting** property value to *false*. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Customizing environment options' space=''].To draw a path faster, use the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Smart manipulator toolbar' space=''].

model_browserTo create relationships between selected elements

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] , diagram pane, or table, right-click an element.
2. Choose Create Relation > Outgoing / Incoming and select the desired relationship type. The Create New <relation name> To / From dialog opens.
3. Do one or more of the following:
  - In the Model Browser, click the element to / from which you want to create a relationship.
  - On a diagram, click an element to / from which you want to create a relationship.
  - In the **Create New <relation name> To** /**From** dialog, click [IMAGE alt='' src='']. In the opened **Create New <relation name> To**/**From**dialog, choose the elementto / from which you want to create a relationship. If you do not know the exact location of the needed element, you can search for it in this step.
4. Click OK .

The **Active Selection** area shows the elements currently selected in the active diagram, table, matrix, map, or Model Browser. It changes with each click of the different elements when you switch to another diagram.

Tochoose one or several elements at a time,do one of the following

- Press Shift and click as many elements as you need.
- Click and drag the mouse pointer to select as many elements as you need.

The multiple selection works in one area at a time. For example, diagram pane, table, matrix, relation map, or Model Browser. If you need to select elements from several locations, you need to pin your current selection.

The **Pinned Selection** area shows the elements you choose to pin from the **Active Selection**. This is needed when you want to select elements from different locations (for example, you need to select elements from different diagrams, or from the diagram and Model Browser). In this case, you should pin each selection.

specificationTo create a relationship in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']

1. Open the element's Specification window and select the Relations property group.
2. Click the Create Outgoing button or Create Incoming button and then select the desired relation type from the list.
3. In the Create New <relation name> To/ From dialog select an element to/ from which you want to create a relation and click OK . The relation appears in the Relations property group.

When the relationship is created, you can visually identify its direction (source and target elements) under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space=''] >**Relations**.

The arrow between source and target elements appears if the path is created using only directed relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="excerpt" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bf87a50f-2022-469d-8621-e1ab9dd02b81"><ac:parameter ac:name="atlassian-macro-output-type">INLINE</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>You can create relationships between elements in one of the following ways: </p><ul><li><span class="confluence-link"><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link ac:anchor="diagram_pane"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Draw on the diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></span> <span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,102,255);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></span><span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,102,255);"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> </span></span></span></span></li><li><ac:link ac:anchor="model_browser"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[From the selected element shortcut menu]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><span class="confluence-link"><span class="confluence-link"><ac:link ac:anchor="specification"><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[In the Specification window]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span></span></li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="12df0f73-6560-406c-bc32-00b07c59f4cf"><ac:parameter ac:name="">diagram_pane</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To draw a path on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[diagram pane]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li><span>In the </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding the user interface" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Diagram palette]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>, click the needed relationship.</li><li>Select the source shape of the path in the diagram pane.</li><li><p>Drop the path to the target shape.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="9654161d-1979-4e30-ae00-9f595d56c4cf"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>When drawing a new path, the optimal route is chosen automatically (the path avoids other shapes).</li><li>When drawing a path between two elements, those shapes are bordered with a red or blue rectangle. The red highlight indicates that the path can not be drawn between these shapes. The blue highlight allows a path to be drawn. You can disable this highlighting in the <strong>Environment Options</strong> dialog under the Diagram node by setting the <strong>Show Manipulation Highlighting</strong> property value to <em>false</em>. For more information, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Customizing environment options" /></ac:link>.</li><li><p>To draw a path faster, use the <span class="confluence-link"><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Smart manipulator toolbar" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[smart manipulator toolbar]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></span>.</p></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1137a2db-b447-4db1-9f4f-f782c4e97275"><ac:parameter ac:name="">model_browser</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="5aba8b2d-b39a-4243-a195-af680247d699">To create relationships between selected elements</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><hr /><ol><li>In the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link>, diagram pane, or table, right-click an element.</li><li>Choose<strong> Create Relation</strong> &gt;<strong> Outgoing </strong>/ <strong>Incoming</strong> and select the desired relationship type. The <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To </strong>/<strong> From</strong> dialog opens.</li><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="011ad7d3-748c-4780-830d-013258e4ee2c">Do one or more of the following:</ac:inline-comment-marker><ul><li><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bd4d2faa-dbc1-4037-a06e-acb7a7a240ec">In the Model Browser, click the element to / from which you want to create a relationship.</ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li>On a diagram, click an element <span>to / from which you want to create a relationship.</span></li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span><span>In the <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To</strong> /<strong> From</strong> dialog, click <ac:image ac:thumbnail="true" ac:height="21"><ri:attachment ri:filename="add_elements_from_the_model.png" /></ac:image>. In the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d630b714-c2d6-41af-ae2c-fa10eefba204">opened <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To</strong><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> /</span><strong> From </strong>dialog</ac:inline-comment-marker>, choose the element<span> <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="c91d2740-784a-4746-bb8a-438080c3138f">to / from</ac:inline-comment-marker> which you want to create a relationship.<br /></span></span></span></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1ee4b3db-2c93-480a-99ff-2ebeec2038a4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you do not know the exact location of the needed element, you can search for it in this step.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul></li><li>Click<strong> OK</strong>.</li></ol><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="create_new_relationship.png" /></ac:image><p>The <strong>Active Selection</strong> area shows the elements currently <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="175a0936-7cef-4181-89da-4b1ef02a695b">selected <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">in the active diagram, table, matrix, map, or Model Browser</span></ac:inline-comment-marker>. It changes with each click of the different elements when you switch to another diagram.</p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7"><br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">To </ac:inline-comment-marker>choose one or several elements at a time<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">, </ac:inline-comment-marker><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7c3f0f3b-09de-4337-8fa6-4e7e973667d7">do one of the following</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><hr /><ul><li>Press Shift and click as many elements as you need.</li><li>Click and drag the mouse pointer to select as many elements as you need.</li></ul><p>The multiple selection works in one area at a time. For example, diagram pane, table, matrix, relation map, or Model Browser. If you need to select elements from several locations, you need to pin your current selection.</p><p>The <strong>Pinned Selection</strong> area shows the elements you choose to pin from the <strong>Active Selection</strong>. <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="71f937c8-7f88-4df0-8c70-b71778086cae">This is needed when you want to select elements from different locations (for example, you need to select elements from different diagrams, or from the diagram and Model Browser)</ac:inline-comment-marker>. In this case, you should pin each selection<span>.</span></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8c813c97-cf66-48c5-8c29-03fa98a9c2c6"><ac:parameter ac:name="">specification</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro>To create a relationship in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></p><hr /><ol><li>Open the element's Specification window and select the <strong>Relations</strong> property group.</li><li>Click the <strong>Create Outgoing</strong> button or <strong>Create Incoming</strong> button and then select the desired relation type from the list.</li><li>In the <strong>Create New &lt;relation name&gt; To/ From</strong> dialog select an element to/ from which you want to create a relation and click <strong>OK</strong>.<br />The relation appears in the <strong>Relations</strong> property group.</li></ol><p>When the relationship is created, you can visually identify its direction (source and target elements) under the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Containment tab" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Containment tree]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> &gt;<strong> Relations</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="223ba6d2-d116-41cb-b0f8-2c839ac04069"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The arrow between source and target elements appears if the path is created using only directed relationships.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><ac:image ac:title="Identifying the relationship's direction" ac:alt="Identifying the relationship's direction"><ri:attachment ri:filename="relation_direction_arrows_under_containment_tree_relations.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145070 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00253: Creating a report including the Actor, Use Cases, and diagram image names

- page_id: `189145070`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145070/Creating+a+report+including+the+Actor+Use+Cases+and+diagram+image+names
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:26.826+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Linking report templates to the modeling tool > How modeling tool adds its elements to velocity context
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

135588550

135588551

135588549

**On this page**

3

135588551

##### Creating a report including the names of actors, use cases, and diagram images

This section will use part of the Inventory Control System.mdzip file as an example to illustrate how to reference elements in a report. This example contains several packages. For instance, if you want to use the 'Use Case View' package, you need to know the elements inside the package before using it. To do this, follow the instructions in the previous section, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Your First Template' space=''].

The 'Use Case View' package contains three element types: Actor, UseCase, and Diagram that you can name in a template by typing **"$Actor**," **"$UseCase**," and **"$Diagram**" respectively. The following example shows you how to create a report that will print the names of the Actors, UseCases, and diagram images.

To create a report that includes the names of Actor, Use Cases, and diagram images

1. Open Microsoft Word or OpenOffice.org Writer.
2. Use the **#foreach** directive to iterate through **$Actor**, **$UseCase**, and **$Diagram**. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Using #foreach directive to iterate through $Actor, $UseCase, and $Diagram in a Rich Text Format template.
3. Use Report Wizard to add the template to the modeling tool.
4. With the " Inventory Control System.mdzip " project open, open the Report Wizard dialog and select your template.
5. Select the element scope so it covers only the "Use Case View" package. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Selecting the Use Case View package from the model as the element scope to be included in the report.
6. Complete all steps in the **Report Wizard** dialog, and generate the report. The output will be shown as in the following figure. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Shows a partial report output. A full report will print all the names of the Actors, Use Cases, and the Use Case diagrams.

##### Glossary

- Actor An actor models a type of role played by an entity that interacts with the subjects of its associated use cases, e.g., by exchanging signal and data. Actors may represent roles played by human users, external hardware, or other systems.
- Element T he abstract root UML metaclass. It has no superclass in the hierarchy of UML elements. It is the superclass for all metaclasses in the UML infrastructure library. There is no general notation for an element. The specific subclasses of an element define their own notation. Each UMLelementhas acompositionrelationship to itself to support the capability for elements toownother elements.
- Use case A specification of behavior. A use case is a kind of BehavioredClassifier that represents a declaration of a set of offered Behaviors. Each use case specifies some behavior that a subject can perform in collaboration with one or more actors. A UseCase may be owned either by a Package or by a Classifier. Use cases are a means to capture the requirements of systems,( i.e. what systems are supposed to do).

##### Sample model

[ATTACHMENT filename='Inventory Control System.mdzip']250

##### More information about creating report templates

To learn more about working with report templates, visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Your First Template' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Linking report templates to the modeling tool' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Exercise: Building a simple class diagram report' space='MD2024xR2']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141323 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00254: Creating a Structured Class

- page_id: `189141323`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141323/Creating+a+Structured+Class
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:06.554+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Class
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There is a way to create a piece of the model with a single click: [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space=''], with a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Composite Structure diagram' space=''] inside it and hyperlink them. It can be useful for architects and system engineers.The same applies to SysML Block and IBD.

To create a structured class

1. Create a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space=''] .
2. In the class diagram toolbar, click the arrow next to the Class symbol to reveal a list of available elements. Select the Structured Class element.
3. Click on the Class diagram. The class element with a hyperlink to a Composite Structure diagram is created.
4. Select the Class in the Browser to see its structure. To do this, choose Select in Containment Tree on the diagram pane from the Class [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Shortcut menu' space='CDH185'] [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-06-21 at 6.19.54 PM.png']

In the Browser, you can see the created Class with the Composite Structure diagram inside it. The Class is linked with the diagram. It means that after double-clicking the diagram, or the Browser, the Composite Structure diagram will opened.

#### TIP: Hyperlinks

Hyperlinks

For more information about hyperlinks, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Defining hyperlinks' space=''].

The names of the Class and the Composite Structure diagram are synchronized. Type a name for the Class, for example, *Library Book*, and the name of the diagram is automatically changed to *Library Book* and vice versa. This is synchronization of a diagram name and its context name.

#### TIP: Diagram Name and Context Name

Diagram Name and Context Name

For more information about diagram name and its context synchronization, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Diagram name and its context name synchronization' space=''].

#### PANEL: Related References

Related References

true[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='']

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class Diagram Wizard' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Behavior' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

It can be useful for architects and system engineers. </span>The same applies to SysML Block and IBD.</p><p><br /></p><p>To create a structured class</p><hr /><ol><li>Create a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Class diagram" /></ac:link>.</li><li>In the class diagram toolbar, click the arrow next to the Class symbol to reveal a list of available elements. Select the Structured Class element.</li><li>Click on the Class diagram. ## PAGE 00255: Creating a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (DOCX)

- page_id: `189145094`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145094/Creating+a+template+for+a+Rich+Text+Format+or+OpenDocument+Text+document+or+Microsoft+Word+Document+DOCX
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:27.513+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Linking report templates to the modeling tool > Supported file types
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1358508562

1358508564

1358530037

**On this page**

3

1358508564

#### Creating a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (DOCX)

You can create a document in .rtf, .odt, or .docx file format and add it to the modeling tool as a report template.

To create a template for a Rich Text Format or OpenDocument Text document, or Microsoft Word Document (*DOCX*)

1. Open OpenOffice.org Writer or Microsoft Word for an *.odt , *.rtf , or *.docx file. (To save a file as an *.odt file in Microsoft Word, an add-on is required. You can get the add-on at [http://odf-converter.sourceforge.net/](http://odf-converter.sourceforge.net/) .)
2. Add the Velocity Template Language codes to the template file.
3. Save the template file.
4. Add the template to the modeling tool.

When generating a document, Report Wizard will apply the text format used in writing the Velocity code.

For instance, when typing:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

the generated output will be:

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

#### Glossary

- Velocity A Java-based template engine. It permits anyone to use a simple, yet powerful, template language to reference objects defined in Java code . Velocity is a project of the[Apache Software Foundation](http://www.apache.org/), charged with the creation and maintenance of open-source software related to the[Apache Velocity Engine](http://velocity.apache.org/engine/index.html). All software created at the Velocity project is available under the[Apache Software License](http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0.txt)and free of charge for the public.

#### More information about working with report templates in Report Wizard

To learn more about working with report templates, visit the following pages:

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145080 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00256: Creating a template for an HTML, a text, an XML, or a DocBook document

- page_id: `189145080`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145080/Creating+a+template+for+an+HTML+a+text+an+XML+or+a+DocBook+document
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:27.159+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Linking report templates to the modeling tool > Supported file types
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To create a template for an *HTML*, a *text*, an *XML*, or a *DocBook* document

1. Open a text editor.
2. Add the code to the text file.
3. Save the template file (with a correct filename extension).
4. Add the template to Report Wizard.

- When you generate a report from a particular template, Report Wizard will automatically create an HTML , text , an XML , or a DocBook document depending on a given template filename extension.
- The #forrow , #forcol , and #forpage directives do not support HTML , text, XML , and DocBook format files.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To create a template for an <em>HTML</em>, a <em>text</em>, an <em>XML</em>, or a <em>DocBook</em> document</p><hr /><ol><li>Open a text editor.</li><li>Add the code to the text file.</li><li>Save the template file (with a correct filename extension).</li><li>Add the template to Report Wizard.<br /> </li></ol><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6853e0b9-8381-4096-9c74-cccd903d3c91"><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li>When you generate a report from a particular template, Report Wizard will automatically create an <em>HTML</em>, <em>text</em>, an <em>XML</em>, or a <em>DocBook</em> document depending on a given template filename extension.</li><li>The <strong>#forrow</strong>,<strong> #forcol</strong>, and <strong>#forpage</strong> directives do not support <em>HTML</em>, <em>text, XML</em>, and <em>DocBook</em> format files.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145085 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00257: Creating a template for an OpenDocument Presentation (ODP) or Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (PPTX)

- page_id: `189145085`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145085/Creating+a+template+for+an+OpenDocument+Presentation+ODP+or+Microsoft+PowerPoint+presentation+PPTX
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:27.356+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Template Creation Tutorial > Linking report templates to the modeling tool > Supported file types
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1351510228

1351510229

1351510227

**On this page**

3

1351510229

To create a template for an OpenDocument Presentation (ODP) or Microsoft PowerPoint presentation (*PPTX*)

1. Open OpenOffice.org Impress or Microsoft PowerPoint.
2. Add the code to the template file.
3. Save the template file.
4. Add the template to Report Wizard.

A presentation document is a special document template that does not contain a content order. It uses a text box to insert text as the content of the document. A text box is an image structure (an image structure keeps the position of each image in x, y coordinates). You can change the position of a text box, or place it in the same position as others.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Writing VTL in a text box in [OpenOffice.org](http://OpenOffice.org) Impress.

The following are the rules that you need to follow when using the **#if,****#elseif**, **#else**, **#foreach**, **#forpage**, and **#macro** directives in **.odp* and *.pptx* documents:

- Statement VTL statements that maintain a sequence order, such as **#if** and **#foreach**, must begin and end the statements inside the same text box. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of multi-line statement in ODP. Do not split the statements into several text boxes, as this will cause an error. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Invalid use of a multi-line statement in ODP.
- #macro statement A #macro statement must be declared in the same text box. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of macro statement in ODP.
- #forpage statement The **#forpage** statement is different from the others, as it does not contain any order. It can begin and end the statement in different text boxes. Its value can also be used in different text boxes. [IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of the #forpage statement in ODP. To use a structured statement such as **#if**, **#macro**, or **#foreach** with the **#forpage** statement, ensure that the statement is in the same text box. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] Valid use of #if and #foreach with #forpage statement. Using an example from the previous figure, the generated result will be as follows: [IMAGE alt='' src=''] The end result of using #if and #foreach with #forpage statement.

#### Glossary

- VTL TheVelocity Template Language(VTL) is meant to provide an easy, simple, and clean way to incorporate dynamic content in a web page. A VTL statementbegins with the # character and contains a directive: set .

#### More information about working with Velocity Template Language (VTL)

To learn more about working with Velocity Template Language (VTL), visit the following pages:

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Velocity templating language' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Velocity variable' space='']

[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Velocity Directives' space='']
