<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141326 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00269: Creating Class Elements

- page_id: `189141326`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141326/Creating+Class+Elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:06.847+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > UML elements > Class
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

A [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space=''] is drawn as a solid-outline rectangle with three compartments separated by horizontal lines. The top name compartment holds the class name and other general properties of the Class (including [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='']); the middle list compartment holds a list of properties; the bottom list compartment holds a list of operations. The property and operation compartments are optional, so you can [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Displaying and suppressing compartments on shapes' space=''] them.

To create a class element

1. On the left side of your model, find the available options under the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class diagram' space=''] toolbar and click on Class.
2. You will see an arrow for a drop-down menu which you can click if you wish to create a different kind of Class element.
3. In any case, either At this point, you should have a Class element in your Class diagram, as shown in figure below. [ATTACHMENT filename='Screen Shot 2017-06-21 at 6.22.38 PM.png']
  1. Click on the element and then click anywhere on your diagram pane, or
  2. Drag and drop, the element you clicked on, anywhere on your diagram pane.

#### PANEL: Related References

Related References

true[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Class' space='']

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='UML elements' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141197 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00270: Creating custom aspects

- page_id: `189141197`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141197/Creating+custom+aspects
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:58.905+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Diagram aspects
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

581366052

581366054

581366053

You can define your own diagram aspects that you want to use in the project. You can create custom aspects in the same way you create a new [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='']. The procedures below describe how to create a custom diagram aspect, e.g., Air, in two different ways:

- .
- 

##### createpd

To create a new aspect on the Profile Diagram pane

1. Open or create the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profile diagram' space=''] .
2. On the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] , click the Stereotype button, place it on the diagram pane, and type its name, e.g., Air.
3. In the Containment tree, click [IMAGE alt='' src=''], and select the **Show Auxiliary Resources** option.
4. Expand packages *UML Standard Profile* > *MagicDraw Profile*, and drag the *aspect* Stereotype on the same diagram pane.
5. Create the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generalization' space=''] relationship from the newly created Stereotype to the *aspect* Stereotype. 
The new aspect is created, and can be used in the project.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The creation of the new diagram aspect on the Profile Diagram pane.

##### createct

To create a new aspect in the Containment tree

1. In the Containment tree, select either a Profile or [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Package' space='']
2. From its shortcut menu , click the Create Element button.
3. In the Create Element dialog, select Stereotype, and type its name, e.g., Air.
4. Open its Specification window, select the All properties mode, and find the Base Classifier property.
5. Select the value cell of the Base Classifier property, click the Edit button.
6. In the Select Stereotype dialog, clear the Apply Filter check box.
7. In the Tree tab, expand packages *UML Standard Profile* > *MagicDraw Profile*, and double click to select the *aspect* Stereotype.
8. Click **OK** and **Close**. The new aspect is created, and can be used in the project. 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] The creation of the new diagram aspect in the Containment tree.

After the custom stereotype is created in the model, the Composite Structure diagram with the custom aspect applied can be found in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space='']**Create Diagram** dialog under the **Aspect Diagrams** group when creating a new aspect diagram. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating an aspect diagram' space='']>]]>

581366051

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the **Diagram aspects** sample model:

- Download [ATTACHMENT filename='diagram aspects.mdzip'].
- Find in the modeling tool: *<modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML\diagram aspects.mdzip.*

/><span><span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">The new aspect is created, and can be used in the project.</span><br /><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_custom_aspect_in_containment_tree.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></span><h6 style="text-align: left;">The creation of the new diagram aspect in the Containment class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span class="confluence-link">Find in the modeling tool: <em><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">samples\SysML</span><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">\<span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);">diagram aspects</span>.mdzip.</span></span></em></span></span></span><span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><span class="confluence-link"><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><br /></span></span></span></span></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189139869 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00271: Creating custom project templates

- page_id: `189139869`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189139869/Creating+custom+project+templates
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:29.066+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with projects > Creating and using project templates
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can create your custom project template that appears in the **New Project** dialog.

Each project template is saved in a separate folder in *<modeling tool installation directory>\templates*. The template folder should consist of the following files:

- A project file containing predefined initial project data.
- An .xml file describing project template data. See the following figure wherein all customizable template data are depicted.
- An image of a dialog title bar icon that is displayed in the title bar of the New Project dialog. The recommended maximum image size is 80 x 80 pixels. Recommended image file formats are .gif or .png .
- An image of a template icon that is displayed in a particular project template category of the New Project dialog. The recommended maximum image size is 24 x 24 pixels. Recommended image file formats are .gif or .png .

Folder name, project file name and *.xml* file name must be identical.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

While creating a project template, you can also create a category wherein your template can be located. If you need to have several project templates under the same category, in the .xml file, define the same category name for these templates. 
 
To create a custom project template

1. If the modeling tool is running, close it.
2. In < modeling tool installation directory>\templates create a new folder for your template files.
3. Paste a project file and icon image files to the created folder. The project file name should be the same as the folder name.
4. In the same folder, create an .xml file and name it the same name as a folder name. The .xml file content should be as follows: <?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?> 
<custom_template> 
<icon>Project_Template_Icon.gif</icon> 
<iconLabel>Projecttemplatename</iconLabel> 
<perspective>MagicDrawperspectivename</perspective> 
<tooltip>Projecttemplatetooltiptext</tooltip> 
<banner_title>Titlebarname</banner_title> 
<banner_description>Projecttemplatedescription</banner_description> 
<category>Templatecategoryname</category> 
<banner_icon>Title_Bar_Icon.png</banner_icon> 
<cloneModules/> 
</custom_template>
5. Save the file and start the modeling tool. Your created project template is displayed in the **New Project** dialog in the category defined by you. Select your template and create a new project. The easiest way to create a custom template is to copy and paste the **Guide to UML****Diagrams****Project** folder with the different name and edit its inner files. This folder comes with the default modeling tool installation and is located in *<modeling tool installation directory>\templates*.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>You can create your custom project template that appears in the <strong>New Project</strong> dialog.</p><p>Each project template is saved in a separate folder in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\templates</em>. The template folder should consist of the following files:</p><ul><li>A project file containing predefined initial project data.</li><li>An <em>.xml</em> file describing project template data. See the following figure wherein all customizable template data are depicted.</li><li>An image of a dialog title bar icon that is displayed in the title bar of the <strong>New Project</strong> dialog. The recommended maximum image size is 80 x 80 pixels. Recommended image file formats are <em>.gif</em> or <em>.png</em>.</li><li>An image of a template icon that is displayed in a particular project template category of the <strong>New</strong> <strong>Project</strong> dialog. The recommended maximum image size is 24 x 24 pixels. Recommended image file formats are <em>.gif</em> or <em>.png</em>.<br /><br /></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="948270d5-a602-4f5f-b37a-d7ad20535f01"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Folder name, project file name and <em>.xml</em> file name must be identical.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;"><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Customizable data for project templates" ac:alt="Customizable data for project templates"><ri:attachment ri:filename="customizable_data_project_template.png" /></ac:image></p><p>While creating a project template, you can also create a category wherein your template can be located. If you need to have several project templates under the <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="870a3c91-07c4-417a-9af2-fc2d699a2559">same</ac:inline-comment-marker> category, in the .xml file, define the same category name for these templates. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" />To create a custom project template</p><hr /><ol><li>If the modeling tool is running, close it.</li><li>In &lt;<em>modeling tool installation directory&gt;\templates</em> create a new folder for your template files.</li><li>Paste a project file and icon image files to the created folder. The project file name should be the same as the folder name.</li><li>In the same folder, create an .xml file and name it the same name as a folder name. The .xml file content should be as follows:<br /><pre>&lt;?xml version=&quot;1.0&quot; encoding=&quot;UTF-8&quot;?&gt;<br />&lt;custom_template&gt;<br />&lt;icon&gt;Project_Template_Icon.gif&lt;/icon&gt;<br />&lt;iconLabel&gt;Projecttemplatename&lt;/iconLabel&gt;<br />&lt;perspective&gt;MagicDrawperspectivename&lt;/perspective&gt;<br class="atl-forced-newline" />&lt;tooltip&gt;Projecttemplatetooltiptext&lt;/tooltip&gt;<br />&lt;banner_title&gt;Titlebarname&lt;/banner_title&gt;<br />&lt;banner_description&gt;Projecttemplatedescription&lt;/banner_description&gt;<br />&lt;category&gt;Templatecategoryname&lt;/category&gt;<br />&lt;banner_icon&gt;Title_Bar_Icon.png&lt;/banner_icon&gt;<br />&lt;cloneModules/&gt;<br />&lt;/custom_template&gt;</pre><p><br /></p></li><li><p>Save the file and start the modeling tool. Your created project template is displayed in the <strong>New Project</strong> dialog in the category defined by you. Select your template and create a new project.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="894dc761-0f00-44bb-810c-5baf55900a9a"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The easiest way to create a custom template is to copy and paste the <strong>Guide to UML</strong><strong> Diagrams</strong><strong> Project</strong> folder with the different name and edit its inner files. This folder comes with the default modeling tool installation and is located in <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\templates</em>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ol><p><br /></p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145490 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00272: Creating Custom Rules for Relationships

- page_id: `189145490`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145490/Creating+Custom+Rules+for+Relationships
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:43.361+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

DSL relationships may have special rules to describe which elements can be connected. Use the following properties to define the relationships:

| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Types for Source | Specifies metaclasses or allowed to connect as the source of the relationship. Types cannot conflict with UML permitted types for this relationship.NoteThe Types For Source property is intended for relationships only. The Customization Target property can be a relationship or a metaclass of a relationship. |
| Types for Target | Specifies metaclasses or allowed to connect as the target of the relationship. Types cannot conflict with UML permitted types for this relationship.NoteThe Types For Source property is intended for relationships only. The Customization Target property can be a relationship or a metaclass of a relationship. |

#### INFO: Note and Example

Note and Example

Subtypes of a specified end type are allowed only if the type is abstract (for example, the Classifier metaclass), otherwise only concrete types must be used. For example:

- If the value of the Types For Source property is the Classifier metaclass, then the Class, Component and other classifiers are allowed to connect as a source of a relationship.
- If the value of the Types For Source property is the Class metaclass, then the Class elements are allowed to connect as a source of a relationship.
- If the value of the Types For Source property is the «Block» DSL element, then the «Block» elements are allowed to connect as a source of a relationship.

In the following table, see properties that are used for customizing non-relationships:

| Property | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Allowed Relationships | Specifies types of relationships that are allowed to be connected to the DSL element. |
| Disallowed Relationships | Specifies types of relationships that are not allowed to be connected to the DSL element. |

#### INFO: Note and Example

Note and Example

Subtypes of specified allowed or disallowed relationships are analyzed only when a specified relationship type is an abstract metaclass (for example,*DirectedRelationship*). In other cases, only concrete types are checked.

- If «Trace» is allowed, subtypes of trace «copy» and «verify» are allowed also.
- If *Association* is allowed, *Extension (a* subtype) is not allowed, but simplestereotyped Associations are allowed. All DSL Associations are not allowed.
- If *Dependency*is not allowed, simple stereotyped Dependencies like «delegate» are not allowed, but subtypes of *Dependency*, for example, *Abstraction* and DSL types based on *Dependency*are allowed.

#### PANEL: Related Pages

Related Pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Numbering Customizations' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with paths and relationships' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145123 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00273: Creating custom tool

- page_id: `189145123`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145123/Creating+custom+tool
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:28.544+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Report Wizard > Appendix A: report extensions
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are a few steps involved in developing a custom tool. These steps can be summarized as follows:

1. Developing a Tool Class
2. Creating an Extension Package

#### Developing a Tool Class

Developing a Tool class requires setting a class path to *magicreport.jar*. You can find *magicreport.jar* from the library folder under the Report Wizard plugin directory. The following sample shows the source code for *Hello.java*.

```text

```

The sample shows two methods following the getter concept defined by the JavaBean specification.

Creating an Extension Package

The extension package is delivered in a *JAR* file. *JAR* (Java ARchive) is a file format based on the popular *ZIP* file format and is used for aggregating many files into one. To create a *JAR* file, you can store *.class with the Java package folder structure in a *ZIP* file format or creating from a *JAR* tool.

To combine files into a *JAR* file (in this case), here is the sample code fragment:

```text

```

In this example, all the class files in the current directory are placed in the file named "*MyTool.jar*". A manifest file entry named *META-INF/MANIFEST.MF* is automatically generated by the *JAR* tool. The manifest file is the place where any meta-information about the archive is stored as named: Value pairs. Please refer to the *JAR* file specification for more details.

```text

```

The example above shows that all the class files in the directory mytool are placed in the file named "*MyTool.jar*".

A complete *JAR* tool tutorial can be found at:

- http://java.sun.com/docs/books/tutorial/deployment/jar/
- http://java.sun.com/javase/6/docs/technotes/tools/windows/jar.html

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>There are a few steps involved in developing a custom tool. These steps can be summarized as follows:</p><ol><li>Developing a Tool Class</li><li>Creating an Extension Package</li></ol><h2>Developing a Tool Class</h2><p>Developing a Tool class requires setting a class path to <em>magicreport.jar</em>. You can find <em>magicreport.jar</em> from the library folder under the Report Wizard plugin directory. The following sample shows the source code for <em>Hello.java</em>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="1d70e644-5f8b-4de6-a310-af5773944920"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[package mytool;
import com.nomagic.magicreport.engine.Tool;
public class HelloTool extends Tool
{
   public String getHello() {
      return "Hello World";
   }
   public String getHello(String name) {
      return "Hello " + name;
   }
}]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The sample shows two methods following the getter concept defined by the JavaBean specification.</p><p>Creating an Extension Package</p><p>The extension package is delivered in a <em>JAR</em> file. <em>JAR</em> (Java ARchive) is a file format based on the popular <em>ZIP</em> file format and is used for aggregating many files into one. To create a <em>JAR</em> file, you can store *.class with the Java package folder structure in a <em>ZIP</em> file format or creating from a <em>JAR</em> tool.</p><p>To combine files into a <em>JAR</em> file (in this case), here is the sample code fragment:</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="86e17c54-b85f-42de-a480-1c3105057a4d"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[jar cf MyTool.jar *.class]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>In this example, all the class files in the current directory are placed in the file named &quot;<em>MyTool.jar</em>&quot;. A manifest file entry named <em>META-INF/MANIFEST.MF</em> is automatically generated by the <em>JAR</em> tool. The manifest file is the place where any meta-information about the archive is stored as named: Value pairs. Please refer to the <em>JAR</em> file specification for more details.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="code" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0c222cf1-44d1-4526-9a37-3acbb179510c"><ac:parameter ac:name="language">text</ac:parameter><ac:plain-text-body><![CDATA[jar cf MyTool.jar mytool]]></ac:plain-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The example above shows that all the class files in the directory mytool are placed in the file named &quot;<em>MyTool.jar</em>&quot;.</p><p>A complete <em>JAR</em> tool tutorial can be found at:</p><ul><li><a href="http://java.sun.com/docs/books/tutorial/deployment/jar/">http://java.sun.com/docs/books/tutorial/deployment/jar/</a></li><li><a style="line-height: 1.4285715;" href="http://java.sun.com/javase/6/docs/technotes/tools/windows/jar.html">http://java.sun.com/javase/6/docs/technotes/tools/windows/jar.html</a></li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145404 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00274: Creating Customization Data

- page_id: `189145404`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145404/Creating+Customization+Data
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:38.465+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In our modeling tool, customization creation begins by creating the following components:

- [ATTACHMENT filename='profile_diagrm.png'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profile diagram' space='']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='stereotype_button.png'] [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='']
- [ATTACHMENT filename='customization_element (2).png'] Customization element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Compounds of DSL customization creation

In the following figure, you can see an example of how customization data is passed to DSL elements through stereotypes.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Example of applying customization rules to DSL element

In the preceding figure, you can see the customization element, stereotype, class element, DSL element and relations between them. The *Company* stereotype element is set as customization target in the *Company* customization element. In addition, the *Company* stereotype is applied to the class element. The customization data from the *Company* customization element is passed to the class element. Thus, the class element becomes the DSL element. DSL element properties will appear in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] and the Properties panel of the DSL element as regular properties.

#### PANEL: Related pages

Related pages

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Stereotype' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profile diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="two_right_sidebar"><ac:layout-cell><p>In our modeling tool, customization creation begins by creating the following components:</p><ul><li><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="profile_diagrm.png" /></ac:image> <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Profile diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Profile Diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="stereotype_button.png" /></ac:image>  <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Stereotype" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="customization_element (2).png" /></ac:image> Customization element.</li></ul><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="customization_creation.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Compounds of DSL customization creation</h6><p>In the following figure, you can see an example of how customization data is passed to DSL elements through stereotypes. </p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="customization_creation2.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Example of applying customization rules to DSL element</h6><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p>In the preceding figure, you can see the customization element, stereotype, class element, DSL element and relations between them. The <em>Company</em> stereotype element is set as customization target in the <em>Company</em> customization element. In addition, the <em>Company</em> stereotype is applied to the class element. The customization data from the <em>Company</em> customization element is passed to the class element. Thus, the class element becomes the DSL element. DSL element properties will appear in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link> and the Properties panel of the DSL element as regular properties. </p><p><span><br /></span></p></div></div></ac:layout-cell><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="panel" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="bc26b85c-b417-4f34-ae40-4e49b382c613"><ac:parameter ac:name="title">Related pages</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Stereotype" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Profile diagram" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Specification window" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140264 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00275: Creating Dependency Matrix

- page_id: `189140264`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140264/Creating+Dependency+Matrix
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:36:49.865+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams > Dependency Matrix
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The matrix element in the model is similar to the diagram element. After creating a new matrix, it appears in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''] as a model element.

To create a Dependency Matrix

1. In the Containment tree, select an element that can be the owner of the generic table.
2. Do one of the following: The newly dependency matrix opens on the right side of the application window.
  - From the main menu, select Diagrams > Create Diagram . Select Dependency Matrix and press Enter .
  - On the main toolbars, click the Create Diagram button. Select Dependency Matrix and press Enter .
  - Press Ctrl + N . Select Dependency Matrix and press Enter .
  - Right-click the element and from the shortcut menu select **Create Diagram** > **Analysis Diagrams** > **Dependency Matrix**. If you cannot see the **Dependency Matrix**, make sure the Expert mode is enabled.
3. Type the name of the matrix.
4. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting criteria' space=''] for the matrix.
5. Click the Refresh [IMAGE alt='' src=''] button. The matrix is created.

You can also create dependency matrices using a [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating new dependency matrix type' space=''].****

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

After creating a new matrix, it appears in the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Model Browser" /></ac:link> as a model element.</p><p> </p><p>To create a Dependency Matrix</p><hr /><ol><li>In the Containment tree, select an element that can be the owner of the generic table.</li><li>Do one of the following:<ul><li>From the main menu, select <strong>Diagrams</strong> &gt; <strong>Create Diagram</strong>. Select <strong>Dependency Matrix</strong> and press <strong>Enter</strong>.</li><li>On the main toolbars, click the <strong>Create Diagram</strong> button. Select <strong>Dependency Matrix</strong> and press <strong>Enter</strong>.</li><li>Press <strong>Ctrl</strong>+<strong>N</strong>. Select <strong>Dependency Matrix</strong> and press <strong>Enter</strong>.</li><li><p class="auto-cursor-target">Right-click the element and from the shortcut menu select <strong>Create Diagram</strong> &gt; <strong>Analysis Diagrams</strong> &gt; <strong>Dependency Matrix</strong>.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0888c118-b6c0-42b0-aff3-6fc4c53e7208"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If you cannot see the <strong>Dependency Matrix</strong>, make sure the Expert mode is enabled.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></li></ul><p style="margin-left: 30.0px;">The newly dependency matrix opens on the right side of the application window.</p></li><li>Type the name of the matrix.</li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Selecting criteria" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Select the criteria]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> for the matrix.</li><li>Click the <strong>Refresh <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="refresh_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" /></ri:attachment></ac:image> </strong>button. The matrix is created.</li></ol><p> </p><p>You can also create dependency matrices using a <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating new dependency matrix type" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[custom dependency matrix type]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.<strong><span style="color: rgb(255,0,0);"> </span></strong></p><p> </p>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140864 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00276: Creating diagrams

- page_id: `189140864`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140864/Creating+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:16.356+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

236646893

236646895

236646894

INLINE

You can create any diagram in one of the following ways:

- 
- 
- 
- 

dlgTo create a diagram using the diagram creation****dialog

1. In the Model Browser, select the owner for the new diagram.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Press Ctrl+N.
  - On the main toolbar, click [ATTACHMENT filename='create_diagram.png'] .
  - From the main menu, select Diagrams > Create Diagram .
  - Open the Content diagram and on its palette, click the Create Diagram button.
3. When the diagram creation dialog opens, in the **Create Diagram** box, enter the diagram type you are searching for. Use the Camel Case, when searching for diagrams:Type the first letters (for example, "CD" to find "Class Diagram")Type lower-case letters (for example, "cd" instead of "CD")Skip the spacers (for example, "cv3" works as well as "CV-3" or "cv-3")Perform a partial search (for example, "ibd" finds SysML Internal Block diagram) [IMAGE alt='' src='']
4. Select the diagram. Press Esc or click outside the dialog to close the diagram creation dialog without creating the diagram.

browserTo create a diagram from the Model Browser

1. Right-click the Package, Profile, Model, or other element where you want to create the diagram.
2. From the shortcut menu, point to **Create Diagram**, then click the diagram type you want to create. You can also create diagrams for multiple elements at the same time. Press and hold the**Shift**key while selecting several elements in your model.

specTo create a diagram from the element’s Specification window

1. Open the Inner Elements property group in the Package’s, Profile’s, Model’s, or other element’s Specification window.
2. Click the Create button. From the button's shortcut menu select Create Diagram , then choose the new diagram type. The Specification window of the new diagram opens.
3. In the open window, type the diagram name and click Close .

wizardTo create a diagram using the diagram creation wizard

1. Do one of the following:
  - On the main menu, click Diagrams > Diagram Wizards , then select the appropriate wizard depending on the type of diagram you want to create.
  - On the main menu, click Analyze > Model Visualizer . The Model Visualizer dialog with the list of available diagram creation wizards is opened. Select one and click the Start button.
2. Follow the steps of the selected diagram type creation wizard.

The newly created diagram is automatically named after the owner. If you create several diagrams of the same type under the same owner, their names are numbered.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145499 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00277: Creating Element - Specific Help Topics

- page_id: `189145499`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145499/Creating+Element+-+Specific+Help+Topics
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:41:44.011+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Using Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1290299781

1290299801

1290299791

Define a string value referring to an element-specific help topic for each DSL element:

- By clicking the Help button in the element’s [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] .
- By pressing F1 , when the element’s symbol is selected.

To define a help topic ID for the DSL element

1. Select the appropriate [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating your First Customization' space=''] and open its Specification window.
2. In the general specification pane, select the Help ID property and type the help topic ID text.
3. Click **Close** when you are done. After updating the customization element specification, you must either reuse the DSL Customization profile in the model or reopen the model. The **Help ID** property is connected to the*documentation.server* [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating path variables' space=''], the default value of which**is *https://docs.nomagic.com*. The opened link is constructed in this way: '<*documentation.server* value> + /display/MD<modeling_tool_version>/ + <help ID text>'. 
Accessing such a link via **F1** or **Help** buttons opens the specified documentation page.

If an element represents two or more DSL elements, the first DSL element’s help topic ID will be taken.

If the DSL type customization does not have the **Help ID** property defined, the help topic ID derives from the more general stereotype customization (ancestor) of the **Help ID** property. If there is no ancestor, the metaclass help opens.

1290299770

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Customization Data' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Customization Data' space='']

The opened link is constructed in this way: '&lt;<em>documentation.server</em> value&gt; + /display/MD&lt;modeling_tool_version&gt;/ + &lt;help ID text&gt;'. <br />Accessing such a link via <strong>F1</strong> or <strong>Help</strong> buttons opens the specified documentation page.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></li></ol><p>If an element represents two or more DSL elements, the first DSL element’s help topic ID will be taken.</p><p>If the DSL type customization does not have the <strong>Help ID</strong> property defined, the help topic ID derives from the more general stereotype customization (ancestor) of the <strong>Help ID</strong> property. If there is no ancestor, the metaclass help opens.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f18f4f39-29fa-411b-8459-36be51cbbd47"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1290299770</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Using Customization Data" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Customization Data" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141898 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00278: Creating elements from other resources

- page_id: `189141898`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141898/Creating+elements+from+other+resources
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:41.357+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

INLINE

It is possible to copy a listed text from other resources (Word, Excel, HTML, etc.) and create as many elements as you need by pasting it. Each item of this list becomes a new element in the model. The basic numbering and bullets are not reproduced in the model.

You can do this either:

43

Avoid names duplication, because elements with the same name cannot be created for the same owner.

##### Creating elements in the Containment tree

To create the elements from the clipboard in the Containment tree

1. Copy a list.
2. Select the possible owner in the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space=''] .
3. From the shortcut menu select Paste or press Ctrl+V.
4. From the Select Type dialog choose the element type. The named elements are created in the model. As example, see the figure below where the Actors are created in the Containment tree by pasting the list.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
Steps of creating elements from clipboard in Containment tree

##### Creating elements on the diagram pane

There are two ways to create the elements from the clipboard on the diagram pane:

- Using **Select Type** dialog.
- 

select_typeTo create the elements from the clipboard on the diagram pane using **Select Type** dialog

1. Copy a list.
2. Click a free space on the diagram pane.
3. Press Ctrl+V and from the Paste Special dialog select Elements . [ATTACHMENT filename='paste_special_dialog.png']
4. From the Select Type dialog choose the element type. The named elements are displayed on the diagram pane and are created in the model.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

paletteTo create the elements from the clipboard on the diagram pane using diagram palette

1. Copy a list.
2. From the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding the user interface' space=''] select the element type.
3. Press Ctrl+V. The named elements are displayed in the diagram pane and are created in the model.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141091 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00279: Creating elements in compartments on shapes

- page_id: `189141091`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141091/Creating+elements+in+compartments+on+shapes
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:49.274+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Compartments
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

To quickly create elements in compartments on shapes, do one of the following

- On the diagram, select a shape and click the Create Element smart manipulator. Then in the menu, select the element that you want to create.
- On the diagram, select a shape. Then next to the particular compartment, click the Create <element name> smart manipulator.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>To quickly create elements in compartments on shapes, do one of the following</p><hr /><ul><li>On the diagram, select a shape and click the <em>Create Element</em> smart manipulator. Then in the menu, select the element that you want to create.</li><li>On the diagram, select a shape. Then next to the particular compartment, click the <em>Create &lt;element name&gt;</em> smart manipulator.</li></ul>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142866 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00280: Creating executable opaque behaviors

- page_id: `189142866`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142866/Creating+executable+opaque+behaviors
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:45:46.800+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Model analysis > Specifying criteria for querying model > Creating new operations > Calling operations from the model
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

337226649

337226661

337226647

**On this page**

337226651

Language of an executable opaque behavior can be OCL 2.0, binary, BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, Jython, or StructuredExpression. Also, it must have the proper number of parameters with proper types.

##### How many parameters can an opaque behavior have?

The number of parameters an opaque behavior can have depends on the selected language of the opaque behavior body:

- An OCL 2.0 expression must have a single parameter.
- A binary expression must declare the exact number of parameters has the Java class, to which the expression body of the opaque behavior references.
- Other script expressions, such as JavaScript or Groovy, can have as many parameters as you need.
- Structured expression can have as many parameters as you need.

##### How to create a parameter for the opaque behavior?

To create a parameter for the opaque behavior

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] of the opaque behavior, Parameters property group, click the Create button. The [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] of the new parameters opens.
2. Enter a name of the new parameter.
3. Specify the multiplicity of the parameter: either select a value from the drop-down list or type a new one. In this case, only the upper bound of the multiplicity is important. For example, the multiplicity [0..1] has the same meaning as the multiplicity [1].
4. Specify the type of the parameter: either select a value from the drop-down list or create a new one by typing directly in the cell. Available types:Built-in UML primitives (Integer, Real, String, and so forth)UML element typesJava classes (java.io.File, java.util.Properties, and so forth)
5. If the multiplicity upper bound is 1, you may skip this step. Otherwise, specify the following:
  - Whether the arguments are unique.
  - Whether the arguments are ordered. If you do not see the Is Unique and Is Ordered properties, select All from the Properties drop-down list in the upper right of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''].
6. Close the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specification window' space=''] .

##### How to access the arguments and other values from script body of the opaque behavior?

The following instructions applies to BeanShell, Groovy, JavaScript, and Jython scripts only.

To access an argument from a script body, you should refer to the corresponding parameter name.

The script body can access the following values:

- Arguments passed to an opaque behavior as parameters.
- Globally defined values:
  - project (current project)
  - application

337251506

**Related Pages**

- Model Elements
- Specification Window

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140725 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00281: Creating Generic Table

- page_id: `189140725`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140725/Creating+Generic+Table
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:45:20.191+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagrams > Tables > Generic table
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

You can create a generic table by any of the methods described in [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating diagrams' space=''].

When creating a Generic Table you should specify the following:

1. Denote the name of the table and the owner package (MANDATORY).
2. Define element types that will be used in the table (MANDATORY).
3. Select elements corresponding to the element types (OPTIONAL).
4. Specify table columns (OPTIONAL). Columns represent element properties, so the table creation could be finished without specifying columns.

You can modify the table after it is created.

This section describes several of the most popular ways of creating a generic table:

43

##### Creating a generic table without using a wizard

Once created, the table can be filled with data using the generic table environment capabilities.

To create a Generic Table without using a wizard

1. In the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space=''] , select an element to be the owner of the Generic Table.
2. Do one of the following: The newly created Generic Table opens on the right side of the application window.
  - From the main menu, select Diagrams > Create Diagram . Type “gen” and press Enter .
  - On the main toolbars, click the Create Diagram button. Type “gen” and press Enter .
  - Press Ctrl+N . Type “gen” and press Enter .
  - Right-click the element and from the shortcut menu select Create Diagram > Other Diagrams > Generic Table .
3. Type the name of the table.
4. Select element types. select_type [ATTACHMENT filename='generic_table_select_element_type.png'] Select desired element types and/or stereotypes in the **Select Element Types** dialog. The **Element Type** box in the **Criteria** area of the Generic Table will display the selected items. This action is optional, so you can continue the creation without selecting any type or stereotype. You will be able to modify selected element types when the Generic Table is created. If you drag an element from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Containment tab' space=''] to the table, the type of this element will be automatically set as the **Element Type** box value.
5. Fill the table with elements:
  - Specify the scope, from which the elements should be added to the table. All the elements created in that particular scope will be automatically added to the table. If the contents of the table have not been updated, try the following:Click [IMAGE alt='' src=''] on the **View** toolbar, in case a smart package is specified as the scope criteria.Make sure the specified scope contains elements that are specified in the**Element Type** box. Element types must correspond to the selected element types!Make sure at least one element type is specified for the table.
  - Create new elements directly from the table. How to craete new elements >>
  - Copy and paste elements from another resource. How to paste elements >>
  - Add existing elements to the table individually. [How to add existing elements >>](https://docs.nomagic.com/display/MD2024xR2/Filling+the+table+with+elements#Fillingthetablewithelements-Addingexistingelementsfromthemodel)

##### creating_with_wizardCreating a Generic Table using the diagram creation wizard

Once created the table is already filled with data.

To create a Generic Table using the diagram creation wizard

1. Open the Generic Table Wizard dialog in one of the following ways:
  - On the Diagrams menu, click Diagram Wizards > Generic Table Wizard .
  - On the Analyze menu, click Model Visualizer .
2. Type the name of the table and select the owner for it. [ATTACHMENT filename='generic_table_wizard_create_diagram.png']
3. Select element types (see ). [ATTACHMENT filename='generic_table_wizard_select_types.png']
4. Select elements. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
Select elements from the model tree and add them into the **Selected** list. Though you can select any element from the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Browser' space=''], only the elements of selected types will be added into the Generic Table. 
If the Generic Table creation wizard is opened from one or more elements’ shortcut menu, the element or the set of elements will be added to the **Selected** list automatically.For more information about the element selection and dialog buttons, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Elements multiple selection' space=''].
5. Specify the set of columns to show on the table. [ATTACHMENT filename='generic_table_wizard_selecting_columns.png'] The list of all available properties corresponding to selected element types is presented in the dialog. If selected element types have tags, they are also displayed in the list. All selected properties will be displayed as columns in the Generic Table. The **Name** property is selected automatically; all other properties should be selected by the user. Select properties you need to see as columns to finish the table.

##### Creating a Generic Table for a set of selected elements using the diagram creation wizard

Note that element types, elements, and columns of the Generic Table will be automatically selected according to theset of selected elements.

To create a new Generic Table for a set of selected elements

1. Select a set of elements you want to add to the Generic Table.
2. From the shortcut menu select Tools > Generic Table Wizard .
3. Follow the steps described in but note that element types, elements, and columns will be automatically selected according to the set of selected elements.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189145451 space=MD2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00282: Creating Inheritance of DSL Customization

- page_id: `189145451`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189145451/Creating+Inheritance+of+DSL+Customization
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-06T19:46:13.654+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > UML Profiling and DSL Guide > Domain Specific Language customization > Creating Customization Data
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

861184440

861184453

861184443

DSL customization is inherited when a stereotype is inherited. So, it is not necessary to create new DSL customization.For example, to create your own subtype of SysML «Requirement», named, let's say, *Performance Requirement*, it is enough to define a new stereotype «Performance Requirement» and add a generalization between the SysML «Requirement» stereotype and the «Performance Requirement» stereotype. As a result, all predefined symbol properties (style) and semantic rules of SysML «Requirement» will be reused on elements stereotyped by «Performance Requirement». In the case where the custom stereotype has its own customization, its properties can be overwritten, inherited, or merged.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### «Performance Requirement» stereotype inherits the customization of the SysML «Requirement» through a generalization.

The rules for inheriting/merging/overwriting customization properties are provided in the table below:

|  | Empty customizations | Customizations with overwritten values |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Original | Stereotype and customization are inherited | Stereotype is inherited, customization is not inherited | Stereotype is not inherited, customization is inherited | Stereotype and customization are inherited | Stereotype is inherited, customization is not inherited | Stereotype is not inherited, customization is inherited |
| customizationTarget | - | - | - | - | - | - |
| hideMetatype | Inherited | Inherited | Overwritten | Overwritten | Overwritten |  |
| keyword | - | - |  |  |  |  |
| representationText |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| standardExpertConfiguration | Inherited | Inherited |  |  |  |  |
| usedUMLProperties | Merged |  |  |  |  |  |
| applyToSource | Inherited |  |  |  |  |  |
| applyToTarget |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| superTypes |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| allowedRelationships |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| disallowedRelationships |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| typesForSource |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| typesForTarget |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| connectionRule | - | - | - | - | - |  |
| quickApplyingFor | Inherited | Inherited | Overwritten | Merged | Overwritten |  |
| hiddenOwnedDiagrams | - | Overwritten |  |  |  |  |
| hiddenOwnedTypes |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| suggestedOwnedDiagrams |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| suggestedOwnedTypes |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| inShortcutMenu |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| showPropertiesWhenNotApplied |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| possibleOwners |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| multiLineTextProperties |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| category |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| checkSpelling | Inherited | Merged |  |  |  |  |
| defaultShapeSize | - | Overwritten |  |  |  |  |
| doNotSuggestAsType | Inherited |  |  |  |  |  |
| helpID | - | - | - |  |  |  |
| preferredMetatype | Inherited | Inherited | Inherited | Inherited |  |  |
| showPropertiesWhenNotAppliedLimitedByProfileApplication | - | Overwritten |  |  |  |  |
| showPropertiesWhenNotAppliedLimitedByElementType |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| allowedDragAndDrops |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| abbreviation |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| additionalContent | Inherited |  |  |  |  |  |
| subElementContentsIncluded |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| doNotSuggestNameAutoCompletion |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| symbolStandardExpertConfiguration | - |  |  |  |  |  |

861184439

**Related Pages**

- Creating Customization Data
- Using Customization Data

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ff4fc0bd-064b-42b5-b9e0-aa332379548e"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">861184440</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="ab7ee9ee-ecd6-4ccf-9dcb-e36f218be637"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">861184453</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="8bf958be-1c56-4b30-916e-9935558e9afe"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">861184443</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><div class="layoutArea"><div class="column"><p><span>DSL customization is inherited when a stereotype is inherited. So, it is not necessary to create new DSL customization. </span>For example, to create your own subtype of SysML «Requirement», named, let's say, <em style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Performance Requirement</em>, it is enough to define a new stereotype «Performance Requirement» and add a generalization between the SysML «Requirement» stereotype and the «Performance Requirement» stereotype. As a result, all predefined symbol properties (style) and semantic rules of SysML «Requirement» will be reused on elements stereotyped by «Performance Requirement». In the case where the custom stereotype has its own customization, its properties can be overwritten, inherited, or merged.<br /><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:border="true" ac:height="194" ac:width="325"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Creating_Inheritance_of_DSL_Customization.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;margin-bottom: 30.0px;"> «Performance Requirement» stereotype inherits the customization of the SysML «Requirement» through a generalization.</h6><p>The rules for inheriting/merging/overwriting customization properties are provided in the table below:</p><table class="relative-table wrapped" style="width: 97.6413%;"><colgroup> <col style="width: 22.1473%;" /> <col style="width: 17.3693%;" /> <col style="width: 12.76%;" /> <col style="width: 10.9612%;" /> customizations</th><th style="text-align: center;" colspan="3">Customizations with overwritten values</th></tr><tr><th colspan="1">Original</th><th colspan="1">Stereotype and customization are inherited</th><th colspan="1">Stereotype is inherited, customization is not inherited</th><th colspan="1">Stereotype is not inherited, customization is inherited</th><th colspan="1">Stereotype and customization are inherited</th><th colspan="1">Stereotype is inherited, customization is not inherited</th><th colspan="1">Stereotype is not inherited, customization is inherited</th></tr><tr><td <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189142001 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
````
## PAGE 00283: Creating inner element as property value

- page_id: `189142001`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189142001/Creating+inner+element+as+property+value
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:38:45.849+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Working with model elements > Editing property values > Cases of advanced editing
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

There are some properties whose values can be their inner elements. Specifying such property value requires to create a new element. The element, which has the property with the inner element assigned as its value, becomes the owner of this inner element. 
Examples of properties whose values are their inner elements: *Owned Comment*, *Owned Attribute*.

To create an inner element as a property value

1. Click an appropriate property value cell.
2. Click the Add button (see the highlighted button in the following figure).
3. Select an element you want to create. The Specification window of the created element opens.
4. Specify properties of the new inner element.
5. When you are done, do one of the following:

- Close the Specification window.
- Click **Back**to return to the Specification window of the owning element.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

To remove an inner element from a property value list

1. Click an appropriate property value cell.
2. Select the value you want to remove.
3. Click the Remove button.

The element is removed from both the property value list and the model.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189141156 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00284: Creating layout template

- page_id: `189141156`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189141156/Creating+layout+template
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:57.944+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Layout templates
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

When you create a Composite Structure Diagram and finish perfecting its layout and looks, you can reuse the same layout in other diagrams of your model by creating layout templates. Create layout templates directly from Composite Structure Diagrams as described below.

To create a layout template

1. Create a Composite Structure Diagram with Part Properties and their Ports or open an existing one.
2. Right click the Part Property the the layout of which you want to reuse and select **Display** > **Layout Template** > **Create Template**.

A layout template is created as the Class or Composite Structure Diagram depending on the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Layout template creation mode' space=''] (see the figure below).

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Creating a layout template from the SysML Internal Block Diagram (Composite Structure Diagram in a UML project).

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">When you create a Composite Structure Diagram and finish perfecting its layout and looks, you can reuse the same layout in other diagrams of your model by creating layout templates. Create layout templates directly from Composite Structure Diagrams as described below.</span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">To create a layout template</span></p><hr /><ol><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Create a Composite Structure Diagram with Part Properties and their Ports or open an existing one.</span></li><li><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">Right click the Part Property the the layout of which you want to reuse and select <strong>Display</strong> &gt; <strong>Layout Template</strong> &gt; <strong>Create Template</strong>.</span></li></ol><p><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">A layout template is created as the Class or Composite Structure Diagram depending on the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Layout template creation mode" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[layout template creation mode]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link> (see the figure below).</span></p><p><br /></p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="creating_layout_template.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Creating a layout template from the SysML Internal Block Diagram (Composite Structure Diagram in a UML project).</h6>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=189140949 space=MD2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00285: Creating Legends

- page_id: `189140949`
- space_key: `MD2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MD2024xR2/pages/189140949/Creating+Legends
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-10-03T20:37:26.978+02:00`
- ancestors: MagicDraw Documentation > User Guide > Diagramming > Legends
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

1294102865

1294102867

1294102864

**On this page**

**4**

1294102866

You can create a Legend right in a diagram or table where you want to use it. After creating a Legend, you also have to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and managing Legend Items' space='']. If you want Legend Items to be created and specified for you, [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Legends automatically' space=''].

##### Creating Legends in a symbol diagram

When creating a Legend in a symbol diagram, you can use either the diagram palette, or the diagram tool bar, as described below.

To create a Legend in a symbol diagram

1. Open the diagram in which you want to create a Legend.
2. Do one of the following:
  - Under the Common group of the diagram palette, click Legend , and click an empty space on the diagram pane.
  - Click [ATTACHMENT filename='legend_button.png'] in the d iagram toolbar and select Create Legend .

After creating a Legend, use the smart manipulator on the Legend shape to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and managing Legend Items' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Legend shape with specified Legend Items in a Requirement Diagram.

##### Creating Legends in a table

When working with a table, you can create a Legend by using the table toolbar, as described below.

To create a Legend in a table

1. Open the table in which you want to create a Legend.
2. Click [ATTACHMENT filename='legend_button.png'] in the table toolbar and select Create Legend .

In a table, a Legend is displayed as a bar right above the table. After creating a Legend in a table, the Specification window of the newly created Legend opens allowing you to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and managing Legend Items' space=''].

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### A Legend in a table with specified Legend Items.

1267814660

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the *Legends* sample model that comes with SysML plugin. To open this model do one of the following:

- Download *[ATTACHMENT filename='legends.mdzip'].*
- Open the model from the <modeling tool installation directory>\samples\SysML directory .

36764562

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Legends' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Legends automatically' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating and managing Legend Items' space='']
  - [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Applying Legends' space='']

After creating a Legend, you also have to </ac:inline-comment-marker></span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating and managing Legend Items" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create its Legend Items]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">. automatically]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><br />Creating Legends in a symbol diagram</span></span></h3><p><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="7adc3d2e-dbb6-4e4f-964f-a76884323470">When creating a Legend in a ac:ref="1f2ef6d9-9170-4bb2-b92e-17a448d7aa01"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3bb9bd71-8464-4d96-bc3b-240cfbd69a33"> group of the diagram palette, click </ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1f2ef6d9-9170-4bb2-b92e-17a448d7aa01"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3bb9bd71-8464-4d96-bc3b-240cfbd69a33">Click </ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="legend_button.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_buttons" </ac:inline-comment-marker><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="3bb9bd71-8464-4d96-bc3b-240cfbd69a33">Create Legend</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker center;"><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="legend_shape.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Legend shape with specified Legend Items in a Requirement Diagram.</h6><h3><br />Creating Legends in a table</h3><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d64f5011-fe97-41d9-8bb1-ac65add1305c">When working with a table, you can create a Legend by using the table toolbar, as described below.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><br /></p><p>To create a Legend in a table</p><hr /><ol><li>Open the table in which you want to create a Legend.</li><li>Click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="legend_button.png" /></ac:image> in the table toolbar and select <strong>Create Legend</strong>.</li></ol><p><br />In a table, a Legend is displayed as a bar right above the table. After creating a Legend in a table, the Specification window of the newly created Legend opens allowing you to <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating and managing Legend Items" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[create Legend Items]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p><br /></p><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="legend_in_table.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">A Legend in a table with specified Legend Items.</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="43d5d029-8ff5-4960-89a8-47b47c24ac68"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">1267814660</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Sample model</strong></p><p>The model used in the figures of this page is the <em>Legends</em> sample model that comes with SysML plugin. To open this model do one of the following:</p><ul><li>Download<span class="confluence-link"><em> <ac:link><ri:attachment ri:filename="legends.mdzip"><ri:page ri:content-title="Sample models" /></ri:attachment></ac:link>.<br /></em></span></li><li>Open the model from the <em>&lt;modeling tool installation directory&gt;\samples\SysML directory</em>.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6bdcf196-e93b-479b-b8d2-ca5b2edf74a2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">36764562</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Legends" /></ac:link><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating Legends automatically" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Creating and managing Legend Items" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Applying Legends" /></ac:link></li></ul></li></ul><p><br /></p><p class="auto-cursor-target"><br /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
